THE MINISTER OF HEALTH AND FAMILY WELFARE; AND MINISTER OF
CHEMICALS AND FERTILIZERS (SHRI JAGAT PRAKASH NADDA):
(a) to (d) A Statement is laid on the table of the House.
α The sign + marked above the name of a Member indicates that the Question was actually asked on the floor
of the House by that Member.
28.03.2025 11
STATEMENT
(a) The current MRP of 45 kg bag of urea is Rs.242 per bag (exclusive of charges
towards neem coating and taxes as applicable) i.e. Rs.538 per quintal.
With regard to P&K Fertilizers, the details of rate per quintal as on March
2025 is enclosed at Annexure.
(b) Under the Urea Subsidy Scheme, Urea is provided to the farmers at a
statutorily notified Maximum Retail Price (MRP). The MRP of 45 kg bag of urea
is Rs.242 per bag (exclusive of charges towards neem coating and taxes as
applicable) since 01.03.2018.
With regard to P&K Fertilizers, the Government has implemented Nutrient
Based Subsidy Policy w.e.f. 01.04.2010 for Phosphatic and Potassic (P&K)
Fertilizers. Under the policy, a fixed amount of subsidy, decided on annual/bi-
annual basis, is provided on notified P&K fertilizers depending on their nutrient
content. The P&K sector is decontrolled; fertilizer companies are allowed to fix
MRP at reasonable levels. The fertilizer companies manufacture/import
fertilizers as per the market dynamics. The Government monitors international
prices of key fertilizers and raw materials and fluctuations, if any, are subsumed
while fixing NBS rates for P&K fertilizers annually / bi-annually to ensure
affordable supply of P&K fertilizers to the farmers. As far as DAP is concerned,
to ensure affordability of DAP to the farmers, in view of the geo-political situation
and price volatility, the MRP of DAP has been maintained at Rs.1350 per 50 Kg
through one-time special package @Rs.3500 per MT and linking the subsidy to
international market prices from 01.04.2024 to 31.03.2025.
28.03.2025 12
(c) The details of fertilizer-wise rates from the year 2013 to 2025 are as under:-
I. Urea
MRP of Urea (exclusive of Central/State levies/GST and charges towards
neem coating)
Year Rs. Per Bag Rs. Per Quintal
From 1.11.2012 to 28.02.2018 268 (Per 50 Kg Bag) 536
From 1.3.2018 to till date 242 (Per 45 Kg Bag) 538
II. P&K Fertilizers.
The average MRP of P&K Fertilizers from the year 2013 to 2025 is enclosed
at Annexure.
(d) Subsequent to the introduction of Neem Coated Urea (NCU), one of the
primary benefits of Neem Coating is that due to slow release, the consumption of
NCU reduces as compared to normal urea. Hence, vide notification dated 4th
September, 2017 it was decided to introduce 45 Kg bag of urea in place of 50 kg
bag. The weight of bags of DAP, Potash and other NPK complex fertilizers has
remained unchanged at 50 kg. Few grades of complex fertilizers with nutrients
over and above N, P, K& S (with specific requirements) are sold in 40 kg bags.

(cid:174)ी बृज(cid:198)े (cid:254) िसहं ओला : अ(cid:197)य(cid:177) महोदय, िकसान(cid:332) स े वायदा िकया गया था िक उनक(cid:236) आय दोगुनी
करग(cid:164) े । वह तो िकया नह(cid:233), अलब(cid:176)ा, उव(cid:170)रक(cid:332) क(cid:236) क(cid:236)मत(cid:164) बह(cid:242)त भारी तादाद म(cid:164) बढ़ा दी गय(cid:233) । अ(cid:197)य(cid:177)
महोदय, इस फरवरी म(cid:164) यू(cid:229)रया क(cid:236) क(cid:236)मत म(cid:164) दो (cid:255)ितशत क(cid:236) विृ (cid:294) कर दी गयी और डीएपी, िजसक(cid:236)
क(cid:236)मत अंतररा(cid:213)(cid:367)ीय बाजार म(cid:164) िगर रही ह,ै 550 डॉलर तक उसक(cid:236) क(cid:236)मत आ गयी ह ै ।
माननीय अ(cid:197)य(cid:177): ओला साहब, आपका (cid:255)(cid:307) (cid:179)या है?
(cid:174)ी बृज(cid:198)े (cid:254) िसहं ओला : मेरा सरकार स े आ(cid:250)ह ह ै िक (cid:179)या सरकार बढ़ती ह(cid:242)ई यू(cid:229)रया और अ(cid:198)य उव(cid:170)रक(cid:332)
क(cid:236) बढ़ती क(cid:236)मत(cid:332) पर कोई लगाम लगाने का िवचार रखती ह?ै यिद हां, तो (cid:179)या कदम उठाए ह(cid:167) और (cid:179)या
कदम उठान े जा रहे ह(cid:167)?
(cid:174)ी जगत (cid:255)काश नड्डा : माननीय अ(cid:197)य(cid:177) जी, माननीय सद(cid:214)य ने कुछ आरोिपत करते ह(cid:242)ए (cid:255)(cid:307) पूछा
है िक सरकार उव(cid:170)रक के बारे म(cid:164) गंभीर नह(cid:233) है और िकसान(cid:332) के बारे म(cid:164) गंभीर नह(cid:233) है । म (cid:167) सबसे पहल े तो
यह बताना चाहता ह(cid:243)ं िक यह (cid:255)धान मं(cid:253)ी मोदी जी क(cid:236) सरकार है, िजसम(cid:164) उव(cid:170)रक क(cid:236) (cid:320)ि(cid:310) से जो भी संभव
(cid:255)यास थ,े वे सारे (cid:255)यास िकए गए ह(cid:167) । 360 लाख मीि(cid:367)क टन यू(cid:229)रया का उपयोग होता है । इस 360
मीि(cid:367)क टन के बारे म(cid:164) इ(cid:198)ह(cid:332)ने (cid:255)(cid:307) पूछा था और म(cid:167)ने उनको जवाब भी िदया है िक 01.11.12 म (cid:164) 268
(cid:321)पये (cid:255)ित 50 िकलो(cid:250)ाम बैग वष(cid:170) 2018 तक था । वह आज के िदन म(cid:164), वष(cid:170) 2018 से लेकर 242-45

11.7% क(cid:236) दशक(cid:236)य विृ (cid:294) दर के आधार पर, भारत सरकार न े लाभाथ(cid:234) आधार को संशोिधत कर 12

को, सरकार न े 4.5 करोड़ प(cid:229)रवार(cid:332) स े संबंिधत 70 वष(cid:170) और उसस े अिधक आय ु के लगभग 6 करोड़
व(cid:229)र(cid:311) नाग(cid:229)रक(cid:332) को, चाहे उनक(cid:236) सामािजक-आिथ(cid:170)क ि(cid:214)थित कुछ भी हो, (cid:255)ित वष(cid:170) ₹5 लाख तक का
म(cid:201)ु त उपचार लाभ (cid:255)दान करन े के िलए एबी-पीएमजेएवाईका िव(cid:214)तार िकया।
एचबीपी का यौि(cid:265)क(cid:236)करण

िकया गया है। श(cid:321)ु आत म,(cid:164) इस योजना म (cid:164) 1,359 पैके ज शािमल थ,े लेिकन (cid:214)वा(cid:214)(cid:195)य लाभ पैके ज
(एचबीपी) 2022 म(cid:164) अब 27 िचिक(cid:194)सा िवशेष(cid:178)ताओ ंम(cid:164) 1,961 पैके ज शािमल ह(cid:167)। कुल 365 नई उपचार
(cid:255)ि(cid:248)याए ँजोड़ी गई ह(cid:167) और 832 पैके ज(cid:332) क(cid:236) दर(cid:332) म(cid:164) विृ (cid:294) क(cid:236) गई है। इनम(cid:164) पहल े स े कवर िकए गए िकडनी

श(cid:322)ु क(cid:236) गई ह(cid:167)।

THE MINISTER OF EXTERNAL AFFAIRS (DR. SUBRAHMANYAM
JAISHANKAR):
(a) to (d) : A Statement is laid on the table of the House.
STATEMENT
(a) to (d) The Government of India, has regularly come across reports of atrocities
committed against the minority communities in Pakistan, including various
incidents of intimidation, persecution, killing, abduction, forced conversions and
forced marriages.
It is the responsibility of the Government of Pakistan to discharge its
constitutional obligations towards its citizens, including those from the minority
communities. Based on the reports of atrocities against minorities in Pakistan, the
Government of India has, from time to time, taken up the matter with the
Government of Pakistan and asked it to take steps to protect and to promote the
safety, security and the well being of its minority communities.
India has, on several occasions, highlighted the conditions of minorities and
violation of their human rights in Pakistan, including at the United Nations Human
Rights Council in Geneva.
(cid:174)ी अ(cid:321)ण कु मार सागर : माननीय अ(cid:197)य(cid:177) महोदय, म(cid:167) आपके (cid:255)ित आभार (cid:211)य(cid:265) करना चाहता ह(cid:243)ं िक
आपने (cid:255)(cid:307) काल म(cid:164) मुझ े (cid:255)(cid:307) पूछन े का अवसर िदया ।
28.03.2025 50
मा(cid:198)यवर, वष(cid:170) 1947 का िवभाजन जनसं(cid:180)या पर नह(cid:233), बि(cid:208)क भूिम पर आधा(cid:229)रत था और भारत
एवं पािक(cid:214)तान के बीच मूल समझौते म(cid:164) वहां के अ(cid:208)पस(cid:180)ं यक(cid:332) क(cid:236) सरु (cid:177)ा (cid:255)मुख (cid:322)प से शािमल थी ।
लेिकन, पािक(cid:214)तान अपने िनमा(cid:170)ण के बाद इस िस(cid:294)ांत को बनाए रखने म(cid:164) पूरी तरह से िवफल रहा है,
िजसक(cid:236) वजह से वहां पर हमार ेिह(cid:198) द ू भाइय(cid:332) का उ(cid:194)पीड़न आज भी िकया जा रहा है । माननीय (cid:255)धानमं(cid:253)ी
(cid:174)ी नर(cid:198)े (cid:254) मोदी जी के के (cid:198)(cid:254)ीय नेत(cid:194)ृ व म(cid:164) िवदेश म(cid:253)ं ालय ने िवदेश म(cid:164) रहने वाले िह(cid:198) द ूसमदु ाय के अिधकार(cid:332)
और िहत(cid:332) क(cid:236) र(cid:177)ा करने म(cid:164) मह(cid:194)वपूण(cid:170) भूिमका िनभाई ह ै । इसके िलए म(cid:167) माननीय िवदेश म(cid:253)ं ी (cid:174)ी एस.
जयशंकर जी का हािद(cid:170)क आभार (cid:255)कट करता ह(cid:243)ं । लिे कन, पािक(cid:214)तान म(cid:164) हमार ेधािम(cid:170)क मंिदर(cid:332) को तोड़ना
और उ(cid:198)ह(cid:164) नुकसान पह(cid:242)ंचाना, िह(cid:198)दू भाइय(cid:332) क(cid:236) ह(cid:194)या करना और उ(cid:198)ह(cid:164) जबरन धम(cid:170) प(cid:229)रवत(cid:170)न के िलए
मजबूर करना एक गंभीर िचंता का िवषय है ।
माननीय अ(cid:197)य(cid:177) : आपका (cid:255)(cid:307) (cid:179)या है?
(cid:174)ी अ(cid:321)ण कु मार सागर : (cid:179)या िवदेश म(cid:253)ं ी जी यह बताने क(cid:236) कृपा करग(cid:164) े िक सरकार को पािक(cid:214)तान म(cid:164)
अ(cid:208)पस(cid:180)ं यक समदु ाय(cid:332) के लोग(cid:332) के िव(cid:321)(cid:294) लगातार िकए जा रहे जघ(cid:198)य अपराध(cid:332) क(cid:236) कोई जानकारी है?
DR. SUBRAHMANYAM JAISHANKAR: Sir, I sympathize with the sentiments
expressed by the hon. Member. There are two parts to the question: one, whether
we follow what the crimes and atrocities against the minorities in Pakistan are; and
two, what we are doing about it internationally.
The first part of it is, yes, we follow very closely and closely track the
treatment of minorities in Pakistan. As an example, I would like to apprise the
House that just in the month of February, there were ten cases of atrocities against
the Hindu community. Seven of them related to abduction and forcible conversion,
two related to abduction and one related to police action against students who were
celebrating Holi.
There were three incidents relating to the Sikh community in Pakistan. In one
28.03.2025 51
case, a Sikh family was attacked. In another case, a Sikh family was threatened
because of the reopening of an old Gurudwara. There was also an abduction and
conversion issue with one girl from that community. There were two cases
pertaining to the Ahmadiyya community. In one case, a mosque was sealed, and
in another case, forty graves were desecrated. There was also a case pertaining
to the Christian community where a Christian person who was reportedly mentally
unstable was charged with blasphemy.
The answer to the hon. Member is, yes, we track it very, very closely. We
take it up and we take it up at the international level also. Again, to give two recent
examples, in the month of February, our Representative at the UN Human Rights
Council pointed out, and I quote, ‘Pakistan is a country where human rights abuses,
persecution of minorities, and systematic erosion of democratic values are State
policies’. They constitute State policies which brazenly harbour UN-sanctioned
terrorists, and Pakistan is in no position to lecture anyone. Instead, Pakistan should
focus on providing actual governance and justice to its own people.
Our Ambassador in the UN General Assembly also, just two weeks ago,
underlined the fanatical mindset of Pakistan, which he said was very well known
for its record of bigotry. So, we are taking it up at the international level.
(cid:174)ी अ(cid:321)ण कु मार सागर : माननीय अ(cid:197)य(cid:177) महोदय, मेरा दूसरा पूरक (cid:255)(cid:307) हमार े पड़ोसी देश बंगलादेश
म(cid:164) रह रहे िहंद ू भाइय(cid:332) क(cid:236) सरु (cid:177)ा के बार े म(cid:164) ह ै । यह सही ह ै िक हमारी क(cid:164) (cid:254) सरकार बंगलादेश म(cid:164) िहंदओु ं
क(cid:236) सरु (cid:177)ा, स(cid:204)मान और भलाई सिु नि(cid:306)त करन े के िलए हर संभव (cid:255)यास कर रही ह,ै लेिकन बंगलादेश
म(cid:164) िहंदओु ंके िखलाफ िहंसा और अ(cid:194)याचार क(cid:236) घटनाए ं लगातार बढ़ रही ह(cid:167) ।
DR. SUBRAHMANYAM JAISHANKAR: Sir, like with Pakistan, we also track the
28.03.2025 52
welfare and well-being of the minorities in Bangladesh. I would like to inform the
House, Sir, through you, that in 2024 we have had altogether 2,400 incidents
relating to attacks on minorities and 72 incidents in 2025. We have the details of
these incidents. I have taken it up with my counterpart, the Foreign Secretary took
it up when he visited Bangladesh, and this continues to be a matter of concern for
the Government of India.
(cid:174)ी अरिवंद गणपत सावंत : अ(cid:197)य(cid:177) जी, जब सन ् 1941 म(cid:164) पािक(cid:214)तान म(cid:164) जो स(cid:164)सस िकया गया था,
उसके िहसाब स े वहां पर तब िहंदओु ंक(cid:236) आबादी 14.6 पस(cid:166)ट थी and 28 per cent were in East
Pakistan, which is now Bangladesh. Today, whatever information we have
received, hardly 1.6 per cent of the total population of Hindus are there, and every
now and then they have to face religious persecution and violence against them.
Two weeks back, four doctors were shot in Shikarpur. I do understand that
academically and politically also your Government is taking action against it. But
still, we are not getting the results.
सर, म(cid:167) जानना चाहता हू ॅं िक आज क(cid:236) ि(cid:214)थित म(cid:164) उस पािक(cid:214)तान म(cid:164) िजतने माइनो(cid:229)रिटज़ ह(cid:167), िसख
और िहंद ू ह(cid:167), व े िकतन े ह(cid:167), िहंद ू िकतन े ह(cid:167) और उनक(cid:236) सरु (cid:177)ा के िलए कौन स े कड़ े कदम सरकार उठाएगी?
जैस े इंिदरा गांधी जी क(cid:236) याद आती ह,ै सन ् 1971 म(cid:164) उ(cid:198)ह(cid:332)न े ई(cid:214)ट पािक(cid:214)तान को बंगलादेश बना िदया
था, उसी तरह क(cid:236) करारी कार(cid:170)वाई (cid:179)या हमारी सरकार करगे ी?
DR. SUBRAHMANYAM JAISHANKAR: Sir, I would like to inform the hon.

DR. SUBRAHMANYAM JAISHANKAR: Sir, we have responded to the situation
of the minorities in different ways. Many of the agreements which the hon. Member
has referred to actually do not apply anymore in reality because there were
commitments which were made by Pakistan for protection of its minorities to which
Pakistan did not live up. So, what we are doing right now, Sir, is while we are raising
our voice and exposing the attacks on minorities, if there are members of the
minorities who come to India, we take a sympathetic view of them. We have given
them long-term visas. From 2014, we have granted 50,019 visas for minorities who
have come from Pakistan to India.

12.00 hrs
SHRI DAGGUMALLA PRASADA RAO : Hon. Speaker Sir, in the case of
Anganwadi centres, Andhra Pradesh is suffering with problems of poor nutrition,
inadequate infrastructure, and low salaries of the staff working there. It is
commendable that under the Saksham Anganwadi initiative, over 92,000
Anganwadi centres across the country have been approved for upgradation.
However, it is observed that while the national average for upgradation stands at
around 6.5 per cent of Anganwadi centres per State, Andhra Pradesh lags behind
with only five per cent of the centres selected for upgradation, which is below the
national benchmark. Therefore, I would like to ask the hon. Minister whether the
Government will consider selecting a higher number of Anganwadi centres from
Andhra Pradesh for upgradation under this initiative.

“PROMOTION OF MEDICINAL PLANTS”
*409. DR. GANAPATHY RAJKUMAR P:
SHRI MANISH TEWARI:
Will the Minister of AYUSH be pleased to state:
(a) the number of AYUSH schemes for promoting the cultivation of medicinal
plants in the country, State/UT-wise;
(b) whether there is any procurement centre for purchasing domestic and foreign
plants;
(c) if so, the details thereof;
(d) whether any rates have been fixed for medicinal plants in the country; and
28.03.2025 69
(e) if so, the details thereof?
THE MINISTER OF STATE OF THE MINISTRY OF AYUSH; AND MINISTER OF
STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF HEALTH AND FAMILY WELFARE (SHRI
PRATAPRAO GANPATRAO JADHAV):
(a) Presently, National Medicinal Plants Board (NMPB), Ministry of Ayush,
Government of India is implementing Central Sector Scheme on “Conservation,
Development and Sustainable Management of Medicinal Plants” throughout the
country to promote the cultivation of medicinal plants wherein following activities
are supported:
(i) Information Education & Communication (IEC) activities like Training /
Workshops / Seminars/ Conferences etc.
(ii) Forward and backward linkage in supply chain of medicinal plants
(Integrated Component) under which the following activities are supported:
 Infrastructure for Quality Planting Material to raise the planting
material of medicinal plants for cultivation.
 Information Education Communication (IEC) activities to aware the
farmers.
 Infrastructure for Post-Harvest Management and Marketing to
increase the marketability of the medicinal plants, adding value to
the produce, increasing profitability and reducing losses.
 Quality Testing and Certification of raw material.
(iii) In-situ conservation / Ex-situ conservation.
28.03.2025 70
(iv) Livelihood linkages with Joint Forest Management Committees (JFMCs) /
Panchayats / Van Panchayats / Biodiversity Management Committees
(BMCs) / Self Help Groups (SHGs).
(v) Research & Development.
(vi) Promotion, marketing and trade of medicinal plants produce.
NMPB has approved 153 projects for various IEC activities to aware the
array of stakeholders including farmers about different aspects of medicinal plants
like conservation, cultivation, post-harvest management and marketing with the
approved budget of Rs. 12.48 Crore from the financial year 2019-20 to 2023-24
including current financial year.
(b) and (c) There are no such centers established for the purchasing of domestic
and foreign plants.
(d) and (e) Tribal Cooperative Marketing Development Federation of India Limited
(TRIFED), Ministry of Tribal Affairs, under its Minimum Support Price (MSP) for
Minor Forest Produces (MFP) component under Pradhan Mantri Janjatiya Vikas
Mission (PMJVM) scheme, 87 MFP commodities are identified for procurement,
which includes medicinal MFP commodities also. The details of MFPs commodities
along with their procurement rates are also available on
https://trifed.tribal.gov.in/sites/default/files/2022-
02/List%20of%2087%20MFPs.pdf. The detail of MFPs commodities is given at
Statement.
28.03.2025 71
STATEMENT.
28.03.2025 72
28.03.2025 73
28.03.2025 74
28.03.2025 75
TRAINING PROGRAMME ON CHEMICAL AND PETROCHEMICAL
INDUSTRIAL SAFETY
*410. SHRI RAJKUMAR CHAHAR:
SHRI ASHISH DUBEY:
Will the Minister of CHEMICALS AND FERTILIZERS be please to state:
(a) whether the Government has organized a series of Training Programmes on
Chemical and Petrochemical Industrial Safety;
(b) if so, the number of representatives who participated in each of the above
programmes including the features thereof;
(c) the manner in which the said training programmes help the Small and Medium
Enterprises in the Chemical and Petrochemical sector to improve safety
standards;
(d) whether any new policy initiatives are being launched based on the findings of
the said training programmes; and
(e) if so, the details thereof ?
THE MINISTER OF HEALTH AND FAMILY WELFARE; AND MINISTER OF
CHEMICALS AND FERTILIZERS (SHRI JAGAT PRAKASH NADDA):
(a) to (e) Department of Chemicals & Petrochemicals has undertaken a new
initiative in the form of a series of training programs on “Chemicals and
Petrochemicals Industrial Safety” to impart training to workforce of primarily Major
Accident Hazard (MAH) units on “Safe handling of hazardous chemicals at work
place and reducing risk associated with hazardous chemicals". There are 2393
such MAH units as per the database maintained by the Ministry of Environment
28.03.2025 76
Forest & Climate Change (MoEF&CC), Government of India. These units include
Small & Medium Enterprises also.
48 such training programmes will be conducted over the course of next five
years covering all the MAH units. Two persons from each such MAH unit will be
trained. During the Financial Year 2024-25, eight training programs have been
conducted covering 554 industries including Small and Medium Enterprises
(SMEs).
Total no.
S. No of units of
Place Date of Training

tatives

Total 554 963
The details of these training programmes including the no. of participants in
these programmes are given below:
These training programs which are being conducted under Chemical
Promotion and Development Scheme (CPDS) focus on improving process safety
and reducing the risk of hazardous situations arising from potential failures. The
training programs are designed for mid-level executives from various chemical and
petrochemical industries. The training programme combines theoretical lectures
with practical exercises, including mock drills for hands-on experience.
Department has engaged Central Institute of Petrochemicals Engineering &
Technology (CIPET) to execute these training programmes. Domain experts from
various educational Institutes like Indian Institute of Technology(IIT), National
Institute of Technology (NIT), Council for Scientific Industrial Research (CSIR) &
Universities and Industry experts are invited to train the participants on topics such
as Chemicals Safety, Risk assessment, Emergency Preparedness, Fire Hazard,
Process Safety Management, Labelling of Chemicals and Safety Data Sheets
(SDS), Hazardous Waste Management, Guidelines and Standards for Chemical
and Petrochemical Sectors etc.
28.03.2025 78
Based on the feedback received from the participants, Department will bring
about suitable modifications in these training programs to be conducted in future.
REPRESENTATION OF JUDGES OF WEAKER COMMUNITIES IN COURTS
*411 SHRI CHARANJIT SINGH CHANNI :
SHRI ROBERT BRUCE C:
Will the Minister of LAW AND JUSTICE be pleased to state:
(a) the total number of Judges appointed to Supreme Court (SC) and High Courts
(HCs) of the country, State-wise since 2014;
(b) the number of judges belonging to SC, ST, OBC and minority communities
appointed in SC and HCs in the country since 2014, category-wise;
(c) whether it is fact that from 2018 to 19 December, 2022, a total of 537 judges
were appointed to the High Courts of which 1.3% belonged to STs, 2.8% to
SCs, 11% to OBC categories and 2.6% to minority communities;
(d) if so, the details thereof and reasons for inadequate representation of Judges
of the said communities in the said courts;
(e) the steps being taken by the Government to provide adequate representation
to the said communities in the said courts; and
(f) the details of judicial posts vacant in the courts in the country?
THE MINISTER OF STATE OF THE MINISTRY OF LAW AND JUSTICE; AND
MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF PARLIAMENTARY AFFAIRS (SHRI
ARJUN RAM MEGHWAL):
28.03.2025 79
(a) to (f) Details regarding appointment of Judges in the Supreme Court and the
High Courts since 2014 are enclosed as Statement-I. Details regarding sanctioned
strength, working strength and vacancy of Judges in the Supreme Court and High
Court are enclosed as Statement-II.
Appointment of Judges to the Supreme Court and High Courts is made under
Articles 124, 217 and 224 of the Constitution of India, which do not provide for
reservation for any caste or class of persons. Therefore, category-wise data
pertaining to representation of any caste or class of persons among the Judges of
Supreme Court and the High Courts is not centrally available. Since 2018, the
recommendees for the post of High Court Judges are required to provide details
regarding their social background in the prescribed format (prepared in consultation
with the Supreme Court). Hence, since 2018 the data on appointees to that extent
is available. As per the information provided by the recommendees, out of 540
Judges appointed from 2018 till 31.12.2022, 15 belong to SC category, 7 belong to
ST category, 57 belong to OBC category and 27 belong to minority category.
As per the Memorandum of Procedure (MoP), the responsibility for initiation
of proposals for appointment of Judges in the Supreme Court vests with the Chief
Justice of India, while the responsibility for initiation of proposals for appointment
of Judges in the High Courts vests with the Chief Justice of the concerned High
Court. However, the Government is committed to enhancing social diversity in
judiciary and has been requesting the Chief Justices of High Courts that while
sending proposals for appointment of Judges, due consideration be given to
28.03.2025 80
suitable candidates belonging to Scheduled Castes, Scheduled Tribes, Other
Backward Classes, Minorities and Women to ensure social diversity in the
appointment of Judges in High Courts. Only those persons who are recommended
by the Supreme Court Collegium, are appointed as Judges of the Supreme Court
and High Court
28.03.2025 81

DATA ON DEATHS DUE TO DISEASES
*414. DR. THOL THIRUMAAVALAVAN:
Will the Minister of HEALTH AND FAMILY WELFARE be pleased to state:
(a) whether the Government has data on the number of cases and related deaths
due to Dengue, Zika virus, Chikungunya and Yellow Fever in the country;
(b) if so, the details thereof;
(c) whether the Government is aware of the Project Wolbachia to control diseases
like Dengue, Zika Fever, Chikungunya and Yellow Fever, etc., if so, the details
thereof; and
(d) whether the Government has any project in hand or has any plan to implement
Project Wolbachia and if so, the details thereof?
THE MINISTER OF HEALTH AND FAMILY WELFARE; AND MINISTER OF
CHEMICALS AND FERTILIZERS (SHRI JAGAT PRAKASH NADDA):
(a) and (b) The reported number of cases and deaths due to Dengue,
Chikungunya and Zika virus in the country during 2024 are as under:
Number of cases and deaths due to Dengue, Chikungunya and Zika
virus in the country during 2024
Dengue Chikungunya Zika
Cases Death Cases Death Cases Death
2,33,400 236 2,31,167 0 83 0
28.03.2025 93
Source :- National Centre for Vector Borne Disease Control (NCVBDC) and Integrated Disease
Surveillance Programme (IDSP).
No case or death due to Yellow fever has been reported so far.
(c) and (d) Wolbachia is considered to be an alternate method to control Dengue
and Chikungunya. The Indian Council of Medical Research- Vector Control
Research Centre (ICMR-VCRC) maintains Wolbachia transfected Aedes aegypti
mosquito. It has carried out laboratory studies on fitness characteristics on these
Wolbachia transfected Aedes aegypti. As reported by ICMR, no project has been
undertaken for field release of Wolbachia.
SETTING UP OF MEGA PARKS FOR PROMOTION OF AYURVEDIC
PRODUCTS
*415. DR. AMOL RAMSING KOLHE:
SHRI BHASKAR MURLIDHAR BHAGARE:
Will the Minister of AYUSH be pleased to state:
(a) the steps taken by the Government to establish mega parks for the production,
processing and promotion of Ayurvedic products along with the key objectives
of these parks;
(b) the major challenges faced in setting up of these parks and the steps being
taken to address these challenges;
28.03.2025 94
(c) whether the Government has conducted any feasibility studies or consultations
with stakeholders regarding the establishment of these mega parks and if so,
the details thereof and the key findings thereof;
(d) the anticipated impact of these mega parks on the domestic and international
market for Ayurvedic products;
(e) the timeline set for operationalizing these mega parks and the strategy for
integrating them with existing AYUSH initiatives;
(f) the criteria and parameters used by the Government to select locations for
setting up these mega parks;
(g) the measures being implemented to ensure that Ayurvedic products
manufactured in these parks adhere to global quality standards; and
(h) whether the State Government of Maharashtra has expressed interest in
hosting such parks and if so, the details of proposals received and approvals
granted thereto?
THE MINISTER OF STATE OF THE MINISTRY OF AYUSH; AND MINISTER OF
STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF HEALTH AND FAMILY WELFARE (SHRI
PRATAPRAO GANPATRAO JADHAV):
(a) to (h) At present, Ministry of Ayush has no plan for establishing Mega Parks for
the production, processing and promotion of Ayurvedic products. However, the
Government has undertaken the following steps to ensure that Ayurvedic products
manufactured in India meet global quality standards:
28.03.2025 95
i. Pharmacopoeia Commission for Indian Medicine &
Homoeopathy (PCIM&H), a subordinate office under the Ministry of Ayush lays
down the Formulary specifications and Pharmacopoeial Standards for Ayurvedic
drugs, which serve as official compendia for ascertaining the Quality Control
(identity, purity and strength) of the Ayurvedic drugs, included therein, as per Drugs
& Cosmetics Act, 1940 and Rules 1945, thereunder.
ii. The MoU was signed on 22.01.2025 between Pharmacopoeia Commission
for Indian Medicine & Homeopathy, Ministry of Ayush and Indonesian Food and
Drug Authority of the republic of Indonesia on cooperation in the field of traditional
medicine quality assurance.
iii. As per the World Health Organization (WHO) certification scheme, the
Central Drugs Standard Control Organization (CDSCO) issues Certification of
Pharmaceutical Product (COPP) to herbal drugs of Ayurveda, Siddha and Unani
(ASU) medicines for the purpose of export.
iv. Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS), the National Standards Body, a statutory
body functioning under the Ministry of Consumer Affairs, Food and Public
Distribution, Government of India, BIS took a significant step by establishing a
separate technical department for Ayush systems for dedicated focus in Ayush
related standardization activity, in September 2022. To guide and oversee the
Standardization activities of the department there is an Ayush Division Council.
Under this Division Council, there are seven sectional committees, one for each
Ayush system. Each committee has its own working groups and panels that focus
28.03.2025 96
on standardization of different areas. Till now, BIS has developed 169standards
covering different aspects of Ayush.
v. The Ministry of Ayush, with the support of the Bureau of Indian Standards
(BIS), has taken the initiative to establish the International Organization for
Standardization (ISO) Technical Committee for Ayurveda and Yoga. This effort has
led to the formation of ISO/TC 249/SC 2 for Ayurveda and Yoga under the ISO.The
secretariat of new Subcommittee (SC 2) has been assigned to BIS (India).
AYUSHMAN AROGYA MANDIRS
*416.SHRIMATI MAHIMA KUMARI MEWAR:
SHRI BHARTRUHARI MAHTAB:
Will the Minister of HEALTH AND FAMILY WELFARE be pleased to state:
(a) the number of Ayushman Arogya Mandirs operationalized till February 2025,
State-wise;
(b) the details of the Ayushman Arogya Mandirs operating in the Udaipur
Parliamentary Constituency of Rajasthan, district-wise;
(c) the number of screenings done for hypertension and diabetes at Ayushman
Arogya Mandirs in the previous year till February 2025;
(d) whether Yoga/Wellness sessions have been conducted at Ayushman Arogya
Mandirs and if so, the details thereof; and
(e) the number of Arogya Mandir in Sidhi Parliamentary Constituency of Madhya
Pradesh and the status of physical resources in the said Arogya Mandirs?
28.03.2025 97
THE MINISTER OF HEALTH AND FAMILY WELFARE; AND MINISTER OF
CHEMICALS AND FERTILIZERS (SHRI JAGAT PRAKASH NADDA):
(a) Details of State/UT-wise number of Ayushman Arogya Mandirs (AAMs)
operationalized across the country, as on 28.02.2025, is enclosed as Statement.
(b) As informed by Government of Rajasthan, a total of 606 AAMs have been
operationalized in Udaipur Parliamentary Constituency. District-wise details of
which are as follows:
S.No. District No. of AAMs operational
1 Udaipur 437
2 Salumber 116
3 Pratapgarh 30
4 Dungarpur 23
(c) As per AAM portal, 107.10 crore screenings for hypertension and 94.56 crore
screenings for diabetes have been conducted at AAMs till February, 2025.
(d) Wellness-related activities like Yoga, cycling, and meditation are conducted in
AAMs. As on 28.02.2025, a total of 5.06 crore wellness sessions including Yoga
have been conducted at AAMs.
(e) As informed by Government of Madhya Pradesh, a total of 484 AAMs have been
operationalized in Sidhi parliamentary constituency of Madhya Pradesh.
Physical resources available at all AAMs include building, human resource,
essential drugs and diagnostics, IT equipment and internet connectivity. For SHC-
28.03.2025 98
AAM, operational guidelines for AAMs provide for, Community Health Officer
(CHO), Auxiliary Nurse Midwife (ANM) and Multipurpose worker -Male along with
ASHAs in the catchment area. For PHC-AAM, the norms provide for Medical
Officer, staff nurses, pharmacist, Health Assistants and Lab technician. Under Free
Drugs and Free Diagnostics Service Initiative, Government of India provides
financial support to States/UTs for 106 drugs & 14 diagnostic tests at SHC-AAM
and 172 drugs & 63 tests at PHC-AAM level.

OUT OF POCKET EXPENDITURE ON HEALTH
*417. SHRI SHAFI PARAMBIL:
Will the Minister of HEALTH AND FAMILY WELFARE be pleased to state:
(a) the definition of Out of Pocket Expenditure (OOPE) on health care by
households;
(b) the details of OOPE incurred on health care per household in the country during
the last five years, State-wise; and
(c) the steps taken/proposed to be taken by the Government to decrease the
OOPE expenditure on health care by households?
THE MINISTER OF HEALTH AND FAMILY WELFARE; AND MINISTER OF
CHEMICALS AND FERTILIZERS (SHRI JAGAT PRAKASH NADDA):
(a) As per National Health Accounts (NHA), the definition of Household Out-of-
Pocket Expenditure (OOPE) is the expenditure paid by the household/ individuals
at the point of receiving healthcare services. These include all expenditures on
inpatient care, outpatient care, childbirth, antenatal care (ANC),postnatal care
(PNC), family planning devices, therapeutic appliances, expenditure on patient’s
transportation, immunisation, over the counter drugs and other medical
expenditures (e.g., blood, oxygen, etc.); and are net of reimbursements of any
nature (insurance/philanthropic donations etc.).
(b) The National Health Accounts (NHA) does not maintain OOPE incurred on
healthcare per household.
28.03.2025 101
(c) As per NHA estimates, the OOPE as percentage of Total Health Expenditure
(THE) has been declining continuously from 62.6% in 2014-15 to 39.4% in 2021-
22. There has been significant increase in Government Health Expenditure (GHE)
as percentage of THE, which was 29.0% in 2014-15 and 48.0% in 2021-22.
The Department of Health and Family Welfare (DoHFW) has also taken up
with States to prioritize allocation to health sector and enhance their health budgets
at least by 8% of total State budget. The budget allocation for DoHFW has
increased by 102.64% from Rs. 47,353 crore in 2017-18 (BE) to Rs. 95957.87 crore
in 2025-26 (BE). The 15th Finance Commission for the grant period from 2020-21
to 2025-26 has provided Rs. 70,051 crore Grants for health through the local
Government.
The Central Government has taken several initiatives for supplementing the
efforts of the States for providing quality and affordable healthcare services to the
people and reduce the OOPE. Under National Health Mission, the Government has
taken many steps towards universal health coverage, by supporting the State
Governments in providing accessible & affordable healthcare to people. The
National Health Mission provides financial and technical support for improvement
in health infrastructure, availability of adequate human resources to man health
facilities, to improve availability and accessibility to quality health care especially
for the underserved and marginalized groups in rural areas. National Free Drugs
Service Initiative and National Free Diagnostic Service has been rolled out to
ensure the availability of essential drugs and diagnostic facilities and reduce the
28.03.2025 102
OOPE of the patients visiting the public health facilities. To provide quality health
care services especially for the underserved and marginalized groups in rural and
undeserved areas, various initiatives carried out under NHM by the Government in
the country are operationalisation of Ayushman Arogya Mandirs, support for
National Ambulance Services, Mobile Medical Units, ASHAs, 24 x 7 Services and
First Referral Facilities, Prime Minister's National Dialysis Programme, various
activities under Reproductive & Child Health and Universal Immunization
programme.
The Jan Aushadi Kendras (JAKs) under Pradhan Mantri Bhartiya
Janaushadhi Pariyojana (PMBJP) provides affordable generic medicines, including
anti-cancer medicines. Under PMBJP scheme, 2,047 medicines and 300 surgicals,
medical consumables and devices are available.
Affordable Medicines and Reliable Implants for Treatment (AMRIT), initiative
provides affordable medicines including for cancer. As on 28.02.2025, there are
222 AMRIT Pharmacies spread across 29 States/Union Territories, selling more
than 6500 drugs including Cancer at significant discounts.
Ayushman Bharat Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana (AB-PMJAY) provides
health cover of Rs. 5 lakhs per family per year for secondary and tertiary care
hospitalization to approximately 55 crore beneficiaries corresponding to 12.37 crore
families constituting the economically vulnerable bottom 40% of India’s population.
States/UTs implementing AB PM-JAY have further expanded the beneficiary base,
at their own cost. Recently, the scheme has been expanded to cover 6 crore senior
28.03.2025 103
citizens of age 70 years and above belonging to 4.5 crore families irrespective of
their socio-economic status under AB PM-JAY with Vay Vandana Card.

28.03.2025 106
EXPANSION OF CAPACITY OF PORTS
*420. SHRI Y S AVINASH REDDY:
Will the Minister of PORTS, SHIPPING AND WATERWAYS be pleased to state:
(a) whether the Government proposes to expand the capacity of ports in the country;
(b) if so, the number of ports of the country selected under this scheme;
(c) the total percentage increase achieved in the capacity of the selected ports;
(d) whether any ports in the State of Andhra Pradesh have been selected under this
scheme; and
(e) if so, the details thereof?
THE MINISTER OF PORTS, SHIPPING AND WATERWAYS (SHRI
SARBANANDA SONOWAL):
(a) to (c) Yes. The cargo handling capacity of 12 major ports has grown from 871.5
Million Tonne Per Annum (MTPA) in FY 2014-15 to 1629.8 MTPA in FY 2023-24,
resulting in increase by about 87%.
(d) and (e) Yes. The cargo handling capacity of Visakhapatnam Port, which is a major
port, has increased by 53.14% in the last 10 years.

AVALANCHE IN BRO CAMP
4602. SHRI DHAIRYASHEEL SAMBHAJIRAO MANE:
SHRI SUDHEER GUPTA:
SHRI VASANTRAO BALWANTRAO CHAVAN:
Will the Minister of DEFENCE be pleased to state:
(a) whether a massive avalanche has hit a Border Road Organisation (BRO) camp
near Manavillage, Chamoli district in Uttarakhand recently and if so, the details
thereof;
(b) the number of BRO labourers killed/injured/missing in the said incident;
(c) whether the Government has sent any Central team/rescue team to the
affected site toassess the loss of life due to the said incident and if so, the
details thereof;
(d) whether the Government has provided/announced any financial assistance to
thedependents of affected labourers of BRO and if so, the details thereof; and
(e) the preventive measures taken/being taken by the Government to prevent
such type ofincidents in future?
THE MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF DEFENCE (SHRI SANJAY
SETH):
28.03.2025 111
(a) Yes, Sir. A massive avalanche occurred near the camp area of EPC Contractor
i.e M/s S&P VCIPL JV (working agency with BRO) in Mana village, Chamoli district,
Uttarakhand in the early hours of the intervening night of 27th& 28th February 2025.
The camp was located approximately 4 km North of the Badrinath Shrine at
chainage 0.100 km on the Mana-Mana Pass road.
(b) BRO has informed that out of 54 personnel/labourers present in the camp, 46
(including injured) labourers were safely rescued/recovered. Unfortunately, eight
labourers have lost their lives.
(c) Yes, Sir. Apart from Indian Army troops at Joshimath responding as the first
responders, a joint rescue team comprising personnel and assets from the Indian
Air Force, Army Aviation, BRO, National Disaster Relief Force (NDRF), State
Disaster Response Force (SDRF) and ITBP supported by the state and district
administration were immediately deployed with the objective of undertaking search
and rescue operations, providing medical aid as well as to assess the extent of
damages.
(d) Under the aegis of the State Disaster Relief fund, a compensation of Rs.4 lacs
has been disbursed to the families of each of the deceased labourers of the
Contractor. Further the district administration is initiating a proposal for payment of
relief under the Prime Minister’s National Relief Fund. Also, the deceased will
receive insurance amount under the Employee Compensation Policy purchased by
the Contractor.
28.03.2025 112
(e) The Government is implementing the following measures to prevent such
incidents in future: -
 Conducting regular safety drills for BRO personnel and laborers working
in high-risk-zones.
 Coordination with the Indian Meteorological Department (IMD) and state
authorities for timely weather alerts.
 Conduct of safety Audit of all labour camp locations.
 Regular awareness workshops and safety instructions in addition to
contingency and safety drills.

IMPACT OF SAKSHAM ANGANWADI ON MALNUTRITION
4604. SHRIMATI SUPRIYA SULE:
SHRI NILESH DNYANDEV LANKE:
PROF. VARSHA EKNATH GAIKWAD:
DR. AMOL RAMSING KOLHE:
SHRI DHAIRYASHEEL RAJSINH MOHITE PATIL:
SHRI SANJAY DINA PATIL:
SHRI BAJRANG MANOHAR SONWANE:
SHRI BHASKAR MURLIDHAR BHAGARE:
SHRIMATI SHOBHANABEN MAHENDRASINH BARAIYA:
SHRI RUDRA NARAYAN PANY:
28.03.2025 118
Will the Minister of WOMEN AND CHILD DEVELOPMENT be pleased to state:
(a) the assistance being provided by the Government to the State Government to
set up Saksham Anganwadi Centres;
(b) the total number of Anganwadi Centres (AWCs) upgraded under the Saksham
Anganwadi initiative till February, 2025 in the State of Maharashtra and the number
of Saksham Anganwadis established and proposed in coming days in Angul and
Dhenkanal districts of Odisha;
(c) whether the Government has conducted any assessment on the impact of
Saksham Anganwadi on child malnutrition and if so, the details of its findings and
the steps taken to improve the nutritional outcomes under the scheme;
(d) the number of AWCs that have received Smart Learning and ICT-based tools
under the initiative;
(e) whether the Government plans to increase remuneration and benefits for
Anganwadi Workers (AWWs) to improve retention, in view of their shortage due to
vacancies and low wages, if so, the details thereof;
(f) whether the studies have shown that Poshan-2.0 has not significantly reduced
malnutrition 3 levels in certain regions, if so, the details of such findings including
affected States and the measures being taken to address the shortcomings; and
(g) whether many AWCs lack adequate infrastructure despite being classified as
Saksham, if so, the details of such cases and reasons for the gap in implementation
and corrective measures being taken in this regard
THE MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF WOMEN AND CHILD
DEVELOPMENT (SHRIMATI SAVITRI THAKUR) :
(a) to (g) During the 15th Finance Commission cycle, 2 lakh Anganwadi Centres
(AWCs) located in Government buildings @ 40,000 AWCs per year are to be
28.03.2025 119
strengthened and upgraded as Saksham Anganwadis for improved nutrition
delivery and for Early Childhood Care and Education under Mission Saksham
Anganwadi and Poshan 2.0. Saksham Anganwadis are to be provided with better
infrastructure than the conventional Anganwadi Centres including LED screens,
water purifier/installation of RO Machine, Poshan Vatika, ECCE related books and
learning material etc. The infrastructural facilities include the following:
Pre-requisites required Infrastructural facilities includes
AWCs should be in Government 1. LED and other Audio-Visual Aids
owned building with Wifi/Internet facility wherever
feasible etc.
Availability of Electricity/power supply 2. Water filtration system/RO Machine
etc.
Drinking Water Facility 3. BALA and ECCE Learning material
etc.
Functional Toilet 4. Poshan Vatikas
Functional Kitchen 5.Other Components - Rain water
harvesting, Clean storage facility, Any
other item/works as per local
requirement
As on date, 2 Lakh Anganwadi Centers has been sanctioned for upgradation to
Saksham Anganwadi Centres. In order to upgrade anganwadis into Saksham
Anganwadis, Rs. 1 Lakh per AWC is provided as per the prescribed cost sharing
ratio between the Centre and State/UT.
28.03.2025 120
As on date, a total of 14,745 Anganwadi Centres in the State of Maharashtra, 53 in
the district Angul and 779 in the district Dhenkanal from the State of Odisha have
been approved for upgradation into Saksham Anganwadis.
Mission Saksham Anganwadi and Poshan 2.0 is a Centrally Sponsored scheme.
The Central Government is responsible for policy and planning and the State
Governments are responsible for day-to-day program implementation. Government
of India increases the honorarium of Anganwadi Workers (AWWs) and Anganwadi
Helpers (AWHs) from time to time. From 1st October, 2018, Government of India
has enhanced the honorarium of AWWs at main-AWCs from Rs.3,000/- to
Rs.4,500/- per month; AWWs at mini- AWCs from Rs.2,250/- to Rs.3,500/- per
month; AWHs from Rs.1,500/- to Rs.2,250/- per month as per the defined cost
sharing ratio.
Besides, performance linked incentive of Rs.500/- is provided to AWWs and
Rs.250/- per month to AWHs. In addition, States/UTs are also paying additional
monetary incentives/honorarium to these functionaries from their own resources
which vary from State to State.
With a view to incentivize and encourage the Anganwadi Workers (AWWs) and
Anganwadi Helpers (AWHs), various initiatives have been undertaken including the
following:
i. Promotion: Under Mission Saksham Anganwadi and Poshan 2.0, promotional
opportunities for Anganwadi Workers have been enhanced. 50% posts of
Anganwadi Workers are to be filled by Anganwadi Helpers with 5 years of
experience and 50% posts of Supervisors are to be filled by promotion of
Anganwadi Workers with 5 years of experience subject to fulfilment of other criteria.
ii. Social Security Insurance Schemes: Insurance benefits have been provided to
Anganwadi Workers and Helpers under Pradhan Mantri Jeevan Jyoti Bima Yojana
28.03.2025 121
(PMJJBY) for life cover worth Rs.2.00 lakh (covers life risk, death due to any
reason) in the age group of 18 to 50 years and under Pradhan Mantri Suraksha
Bima Yojana for accidental cover of Rs.2.00 Lakh (accidental death and permanent
full disability) /Rs.1.00 Lakh (partial but permanent disability) in the age group of
18-59 years.
iii. State Governments and UT Administrations have been requested to encourage
eligible AWWs and AWHs to get themselves enrolled under the Pradhan Mantri
Shram Yogi Mandhan (PM-SYM) Pension Scheme, which is a voluntary and
contributory pension scheme for the unorganized sectors in the country to ensure
old age protection.
iv. Retirement date: States and UTs have been requested to adopt a uniform
retirement date i.e 30th April of each year with respect to Anganwadi Workers and
Helpers to ensure proper human resource planning.
v. Healthcare annual coverage of Rs. 5 Lakhs under Ayushman Bharat Pradhan
Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana (AB-PMJAY) to all Anganwadi workers and helpers in
pursuance to budget announcement in FY 2024-25.
Various rounds of the National Family Health Survey (NFHS) conducted by Ministry
of Health & Family Welfare since 1992-93 have shown improvement in malnutrition
indicators in children across India. Details of these indicators for children since
NFHS-1 to NFHS-5 are given below:

The projected population of all children up to 5 years in India for the year 2021 is
13.75 crores approximately (source: Population Projections for India and States
2011-2036, National Commission on Population, Ministry of Health & Family
Welfare). However, only 7.49 crores children up to 5 years were enrolled in
Anganwadi’s and registered on Poshan Tracker of the Ministry of Women & Child
Development as per the February 2025 data. 7.25 crores of these children were
measured on growth parameters of height and weight. 39.09% of them have been
found to be stunted, 16.60% have been found to be underweight and 5.35%
wasted.
Further, the projected population of all children in India up to 6 years for the year
2021 is approximately 16.1 crores. As per the February 2025 data of Poshan
Tracker, 8.80 crores children (0-6 years) were enrolled in Anganwadis out of whom
8.52 crores were measured on growth parameters of height and weight. 37.75% of
these children (0-6 years) have been found to be stunted and 17.19% children (0-
6 years) have been found to be underweight.
28.03.2025 123
The analysis of the above NFHS data and the Poshan Tracker data shows
improvement in malnutrition indicators in children across India.

28.03.2025 124
SWADHAR GREH SCHEME
4606. SHRI S JAGATHRATCHAKAN:
Will the Minister of WOMEN AND CHILD DEVELOPMENT be pleased to state:
(a) whether the Union Government has reviewed the implementation of the
SwadharGreh Scheme in Tamil Nadu for rehabilitation of vulnerable women;
(b) the details of the funds allocated/utilized under the SwadharGreh Scheme in
the said State during the fiscal years 2023-24 and 2024-25;
(c) the number of SwadharGreh Centres currently functional in Tamil Nadu along
with the women supported through these centres; and
(d) whether the Government plans to increase the number and capacity of such
centres in Tamil Nadu in the upcoming fiscal year, if so, the details thereof?
THE MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF WOMEN AND CHILD
DEVELOPMENT (SHRIMATI SAVITRI THAKUR):
(a) to (d) Under the overarching ‘Mission Shakti’, erstwhile schemes of
‘SwadharGreh’ – Home for women in difficult circumstances and ‘Ujjawala’- Home
for women rescued from Trafficking, have been merged and are now known as
‘Shakti Sadan Scheme’, which is an Integrated Relief and Rehabilitation Home for
women in distressful situations including trafficked women. It aims at creating a
safe and enabling environment for the women in such difficult situations, to enable
them to overcome the adverse circumstances.
The Shakti Sadan Scheme is a demand driven Centrally Sponsored Scheme,
under which funds are released to the States/UTs for implementation of the
28.03.2025 125
scheme. The States/UTs assess their requirement as per local needs and
proposals are approved every year by the Programme Approval Board (PAB) after
discussion with States/UTs. Under the Scheme, financial assistance is provided
for running of Shakti Sadan in rented premises.
The details of funds released to the State Government of Tamil Nadu for the
implementation of Shakti Sadan Scheme during the financial years 2023-24 and
2024-25 are as under:
Financial Year Funds released
(Amount Rupees in crore)
2023-24 15.17
2024-25 10.54
(upto 26.03.2025)
Presently, 40 Shakti Sadans are functional in the State of Tamil Nadu
benefiting about 1706 women.
INCREASING NUMBER OF BEDS IN WOMEN'S HOSPITALS
4607. SHRI A MANI:
Will be minister of HEALTH AND FAMILY WELFARE be pleased to state:
(a) whether the Government is implementing any scheme to increase the number
of beds in women’s hospitals to provide immediate medical care and essential
medicines to pregnant women in urban areas and aspirational districts of the
country, including Tamil Nadu;
28.03.2025 126
(b) if so, the details of the scheme along with the number of hospitals identified for
expansion, funds allocated and the timeline for its implementation; and
(c) the steps taken/proposed to be taken by the Government to increase bed
capacity in hospitals where the patient burden is high along with the provision
of additional medical staff, essential medicines and maternal care services to
ensure justice and timely treatment for female patients?
THE MINISTER OF STATE OF THE MINISTRY OFAYUSH; AND MINISTER OF
STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF HEALTH AND FAMILY WELFARE (SHRI
PRATAPRAO GANPATRAO JADHAV):
(a) to (c) Public health and hospitals are state subjects. The primary responsibility
for strengtheninghealthcare systems lies with the respective state/UT governments.
However, to address thegaps in the availability of hospital beds and to provide
health care services to pregnantwomen across the country, including urban areas
and aspirational districts, the Ministry ofHealth & Family Welfare, Government of
India, provides financial and technical support toStates/UTs to strengthen their
healthcare systems based on the proposals submitted byStates/UTs in their
Program Implementation Plans (PIPs). The Government of India approvesthe
proposals in the form of Record of Proceedings (RoPs) as per norms and
availableresources.
Under National Health Mission (NHM), Government of India has the provision
for the establishment of a dedicated Maternal and Child Health (MCH) wing within
the premises of an existing health facility including Women's Hospital across the
28.03.2025 127
country including urban area and aspirational districts of the Tamil Nadu. These
approvals are based on the proposals submitted by the States/UTs in their
Programme Implementation Plans (PIP), provided the existing heath facility has the
benchmark Bed Occupancy Rate (BOR) of 70%. In FY 2024-25 support was
provided for establishment / construction of MCH wings to the extent of 92
numbers in various health facilities for which an amount of Rs. 491.24 Crores is
approved. This is approved towards support for ongoing works approved in
previous years and new works approved in FY 2024-25.
As per IPHS norms, Primary Health Centre (PHC) with 6 indoor/ observation
beds is to be established to cover 20,000 to 30,000 population, Community Health
Centres (CHC) with 30 beds to cover population of 80,000-1,20,000, Sub-District
Hospitals with 31-100 beds to cover population of 1,00,000 - 5,00,000 and District
Hospital with 101-500 beds to cover population upto 30,00,000.
Health Dynamics of India 2022-23 report, which is published based on the
data reported by States/Union Territories (UTs), states that there are 8,18,661 beds
at PHC, CHC, Sub District Hospital, District Hospital and Medical Colleges in India
as on 31.03.2023.
The State/Union Territories (UTs) wise details of beds available including
Tamil Nadu is enclosed as Statement.
Pradhan Mantri Ayushman Bharat Health Infrastructure Mission (PM-ABHIM):
Under the Central Sponsored Scheme (CSS) components of the scheme support
for 100 and 50-bedded Critical Care Hospital Blocks (CCBs) at district hospitals or
28.03.2025 128
medical colleges for an amount of Rs.11614.71 Crore have been accorded to
States/UTs for FY 2021-22 to 2024-25 for setting up of 395 CCBs. Under Central
Sector component of PM-ABHIM, 150 bedded Critical Care Blocks are envisaged
to be established in 12 AIIMS and Institute of National Importance (INIs).
ECRP-II:"India Covid-19 Emergency Response and Health Systems
Preparedness Package-II" (ECRP-II) provided support for an amount of
Rs.23,123 Crore for the F.Y. 2021-22. Under ECRP-II to respond to the bed
availability during Covid-19 pandemic, the following sanctions were given to the
States/UTs to improve bed availability in Public Sector Hospitals including Medical
Colleges. The details are as under:
Total 37,834 ICU beds inclusive of 9,873 Paediatric ICU Beds, 7,008
Paediatric High Dependency Unit (HDU) Beds, 20,953 Adult ICU Beds in Medical
Colleges, District Hospitals and Sub-Divisional Hospitals.
Additional 124,859 beds at the existing CHCs, PHCs and Sub Health Centres
(6-20 bedded units); 19,337 Oxygen supported Paediatric beds; Field hospitals (50-
100 bedded units) with 20,102 Beds.
26 Critical Care Blocks (CCBs) (50 bedded) in Hilly States in the Hilly and North
Eastern State/UTs.
Pradhan MantriSwasthya Suraksha Yojana (PMSSY): Under the Pradhan
MantriSwasthya Suraksha Yojana (PMSSY), there is a provision for upgradation of
existing Government Medical Colleges/Institutions (GMCIs) by way of setting up of
Super Specialty Blocks (SSB)/Trauma Centres. So far, 75 such projects have been
28.03.2025 129
approved under the Scheme. Further, establishment of 22 new All India Institute of
Medical Sciences (AIIMS) has been approved under the Scheme.
Under Centrally Sponsored Scheme for ‘Establishment of new Medical
Colleges attached with existing district/referral hospitals’, a total of 157 Medical
Colleges have been approved in the country.

मू(cid:208)य िनधा(cid:170)(cid:229)रत अथवा संशोिधत िकए गए ह(cid:167), क(cid:236) सभी मू(cid:208)य अिधसूचनाए ं एनपीपीए क(cid:236) वेबसाइट अथा(cid:170)त्
www.nppaindia.nic.in पर उपल(cid:202)ध है।
JANANI SHISHU SURAKSHA KARYAKRAM IN ANDHRA PRADESH
4609. SHRI B K PARTHASARATHI:
Will the Minister of HEALTH AND FAMILY WELFARE be pleased to state:
(a) the details of funds sanctioned, released and utilized under Janani Shishu
Suraksha Karyakram (JSSK) during last five years, year-wise and State/district-
wise especially in Andhra Pradesh;
28.03.2025 133
(b) the number of beneficiaries provided free and zero expense delivery and
cesarean sections under the said scheme during the last five years, year-wise and
State/district-wise including Andhra Pradesh; and
(c) the details of sick infants provided free and zero expense treatment provided
under the Scheme during the last five years, year-wise and State/district-wise
including Andhra Pradesh?
THE MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF HEALTH AND FAMILY
WELFARE; ANDMINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF CHEMICALS AND
FERTILIZERS (SHRIMATI ANUPRIYA PATEL):
(a) The year-wise and State-wise details of funds approved and expenditure under
Janani Shishu Suraksha Karyakram (JSSK) during the last five years, including
Andhra Pradesh, is placed at Statement-I. The district wise budget released and
expenditure incurred under JSSK in the State of Andhra Pradesh as provided by
the State during the last five years, is placed at Statement II.
(b) The year-wise and State-wise details of pregnant women benefitted under the
JSSK during the last five years and in the districts of Andhra Pradesh (as provided
by the State) is placed at Statement-III and IV respectively.
(c) The year-wise and State-wise details of sick Infants benefitted under the JSSK
during the last five years and in the districts of Andhra Pradesh (as provided by the
State) is placed at Statement -V and VI respectively.

NATIONAL FUND FOR RARE DISEASES
4610. SHRI BAIJAYANT PANDA:
Will the Minister of HEALTH AND FAMILY WELFARE be pleased to state:
28.03.2025 154
(a) the details of fund allocations for the National Fund for Rare Diseases
(NFRD) for the financial years 2024-25 and 2025-26;
(b) the total number of disorders currently classified as rare diseases in the
country;
(c) whether the Government provides financial support through any specific
schemes for patients suffering from rare diseases and if so, the details of
such schemes; and
(d) the status of the eleven Centres of Excellence (CoEs) established for the
treatment and diagnosis of rare diseases along with the number of patients
receiving treatment at these centres?
THE MINISTER OF STATE OF THE MINISTRY OFAYUSH; AND MINISTER OF
STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF HEALTH AND FAMILY WELFARE (SHRI
PRATAPRAO GANPATRAO JADHAV):
(a) to (d) Health being a State subject, the primary responsibility of providing
healthcare services is that of the State Governments. However, to mitigate the
challenges posed by rare diseases in India, Ministry of Health and Family Welfare
has framed National Policy for Rare Diseases (NPRD), 2021. The policy covers 63
disorders which are categorized as Rare Diseases. Financial assistance up to Rs.
50 lakhs per patient is provided for treatment of Rare Diseases through 13 Centers
of Excellence (CoEs) identified under NPRD, 2021. The fund allocation is made
under NPRD and there is no National Fund for Rare Diseases (NFRD) as such.
28.03.2025 155
The details of NPRD and other related circulars are available at Ministry’s website
viz. https://mohfw.gov.in. As on date, around 1000 patients are registered in the
CoEs for identified Rare Diseases. Rs. 118.82 crores and Rs. 299.59 croreshave
been allocated for Rare Diseases for FY 2024-25 and FY 2025-26 respectively.
TRADE TALKS BETWEEN INDIA AND USA
4611 SHRI A RAJA:
Will the Minister of EXTERNAL AFFAIRS be pleased to state:-
(a) whether any roadmap for the formal talks on trade deals between India and
USA has been finalized, during the visit of Indian PM to USA;
(b) if so, the details thereof;
(c) the details of other areas negotiated for the bilateral trade apart from signing
agreement for purchase of Defence planes from USA;
(d) whether it is a fact that Indian representatives strive for the fair, equitable and
mutually beneficial deal between the two countries, in view of high tariff issue;
and
(e) if so, the details thereof?
THE MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF ENVIRONMENT, FOREST
AND CLIMATE CHANGE; AND MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF
EXTERNAL AFFAIRS (SHRI KIRTI VARDHAN SINGH):
28.03.2025 156
(a) to (e) During the visit of Prime Minister Modi to the United States on 12-13
February 2025, both India and the US resolved to deepen the bilateral trade
relationship to promote growth that ensures fairness, national security and job
creation. To this end, PM Modi and US President Donald Trump set a new bilateral
trade target – "Mission 500” – aiming to more than double the total two-way goods
and services trade to $500 billion by 2030. Recognizing that this level of ambition
would require new, fair-trade terms, both sides announced plans to negotiate the
first tranche of a mutually beneficial, multi-sector Bilateral Trade Agreement (BTA)
by the fall of 2025.
The two governments are in the process of taking these negotiations forward
and are actively working to build a framework for the BTA, which aims to expand
trade across the goods and services sectors, whilst working towards increasing
market access, reducing tariff and non-tariff barriers, and deepening supply chain
integration.
Towards this endeavour, Minister of Commerce and Industry Shri Piyush
Goyal visited the U.S. on 03-07 March 2025 and held discussions with US Trade
Representative (USTR) Jamieson Greer and US Secretary of Commerce Howard
Lutnick.
Government of India remains engaged with the U.S. Administration to arrive
at a mutually beneficial, multi-sector Bilateral Trade Agreement (BTA).
28.03.2025 157
INDIAN WORKERS IN THE MIDDLE EAST
4612 DR. NAMDEO KIRSAN:
Will the Minister of EXTERNAL AFFAIRS be pleased to state:-
(a) The number of individuals from Maharashtra who have repatriated from the
Middle East during the last three years along with the measures undertaken to
rehabilitate them, year wise;
(b) The details of helplines and grievance redressal mechanisms available for
Indian workers in the Middle East including the number of cases registered
and resolved during the last three years, year wise;
(c) The total allocation and utilization of the Indian Community Welfare Fund
(ICWF) during the last five years, with a specific breakdown of the amount
utilized for the welfare of workers from Maharashtra in the Middle East along
with the details of welfare measures provided, year-wise; and
(d) The details of skill development or pre-departure orientation programmes
conducted for workers seeking employment in the Middle East, including the
number of beneficiaries thereof, state wise?
THE MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF ENVIRONMENT, FOREST
AND CLIMATE CHANGE; AND MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF
EXTERNAL AFFAIRS (SHRI KIRTI VARDHAN SINGH):
(a) and (b) State wise breakdown of the number of Indians repatriated from the
Middle East in the last three years is not available with the Ministry.
28.03.2025 158
The Government has established various mechanisms to enable Indian workers
abroad to reach out in case they need any assistance. The workers can contact the
Missions/Posts through various channels like walk-in, email, multilingual 24x7
emergency numbers, grievance redressal portals like MADAD, CPGRAMS,
eMigrate and social media etc.
Pravasi Bharatiya Sahayata Kendras (PBSK) have been set up in New Delhi and
at Dubai (UAE), Riyadh & Jeddah (Kingdom of Saudi Arabia) to provide assistance,
guidance and counselling to Indian workers in distress.
Indian Missions/Posts abroad have also established 24X7 Helplines
including Toll Free Helplines, whatsapp numbers and have launched mobile Apps
to enable Indian nationals contact respective Indian Missions/Posts when in
distress or in emergency situation. Missions in Gulf countries also have dedicated
labour wings which ensure prompt redressal of labor grievances.
Data on Indians who were provided assistance under various categories, is given
at Statement-I.
(c) No budgetary allocation is provided for ICWF. Source of ICWF is fees levied on
consular services in Missions abroad. State-wise breakdown of the beneficiaries of
ICWF is not available with the Ministry. However, the details of utilization of ICWF
during the last five years in the Middle East is given below:
28.03.2025 159
Year 2020 2021 2022 2023 2024
Total 8,58,90,543 7,13,63,876 7,46,93,666 8,33,51,742 12,44,41,156
utilization of
ICWF in INR
(d) The Pre-Departure Orientation Training Programme was launched by the
Ministry of External Affairs in January, 2018 under the Pravasi Kaushal Vikas
Yojana (PKVY) with an aim of enhancing soft skills of Indian emigrant workers,
going abroad specially to Gulf and other ECR countries. Under this programme,
the emigrant workers are given one day free of cost orientation training aiming to
help them to understand local rules and regulations of the destination country and
their culture, tradition, language etc. The training also provide an opportunity to
sensitise the emigrant workers about the pathways to safe and legal migration and
to brief them about various welfare schemes and initiatives of the Government of
India for their welfare and protection such as ICWF, MADAD, PBBY etc.
Since the inception of the PDOT programme in 2018, a total of 1,72,220
emigrant workers have been imparted orientation training till 31st December 2024.
The Centre-wise list of beneficiaries of PDOT is attached at Statement-II.
28.03.2025 160

BUDGET ALLOCATION FOR MEA
4613. SHRI SUDAMA PRASAD
Will the Minister of EXTERNAL AFFAIRS be pleased to state:-
28.03.2025 165
(a) whether the total budget allocation for the Ministry of External Affairs (MEA)
for 2025-26 has been decreased by 7.3% as compared to last year’s
projections as per the report published in the wire.in dated 3/02/25 titled
'Foreign Aid: Who Gains, Who Loses in India’s 2025-26 Budget', if so, the
reasons therefor;
(b) whether the current reduction in the MEA budget is likely to impact India’s
foreign aid commitments in South Asia, if so, the details thereof;
(c) whether it is a fact that Maldives and Sri Lanka are the only South Asian
countries set to receive increased foreign aid from India this year, if so, the
rationale behind the same; and
(d) the details of India’s foreign aid allocations for the last ten years, region-wise
and year-wise?
THE MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF ENVIRONMENT, FOREST
AND CLIMATE CHANGE; AND MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF
EXTERNAL AFFAIRS (SHRI KIRTI VARDHAN SINGH):
(a) The total budget allocation for the Ministry of External Affairs (MEA) for 2025-
26 is Rs. 20,516.61 crore and for 2024-25 is Rs. 22,154.67 crore. The budget
allocation for 2024-25 includes Rs. 4383.40 crore for “Payment to Exim Bank
towards Guarantees invoked against Doubtful Debts”. If this amount is excluded,
then the core budget of MEA for 2024-25 is Rs. 17,771.27 crore. Therefore, the net
28.03.2025 166
budget allocation of MEA for 2025-26 is approximately 15% higher than that for
2024-25.
(b) and (c) No.
(d) Details of India’s foreign aid allocations for the last ten years, region-wise and
year-wise are attached at Statement.

INDO-US DIPLOMATIC RELATIONS
4614. SHRI ANIL YESHWANT DESAI:
Will the Minister of EXTERNAL AFFAIRS be pleased to state:-
(a) the present status of our diplomatic relations with America in the wake of
change of guard there;
(b) whether the executive orders issued by the new US President have any effect
on Indians living in America;
(c) if so, the details thereof;
(d) whether there is any change perceived in the trade with US; and
(e) if so, the details thereof?
28.03.2025 168
THE MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF ENVIRONMENT, FOREST
AND CLIMATE CHANGE; AND MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF
EXTERNAL AFFAIRS (SHRI KIRTI VARDHAN SINGH):
(a) India – U.S. Comprehensive Global Strategic Partnership enjoys strong
bipartisan support and continues to maintain a positive momentum since the
inauguration of the new U.S. Administration on 20 January 2025. During Prime
Minister Modi’s recent visit to Washington DC, both leaders launched the “U.S.-
India COMPACT (Catalyzing Opportunities for Military Partnership, Accelerated
Commerce & Technology) for the 21st Century” – to drive transformative change
across key pillars of cooperation. Both sides have already initiated necessary action
to implement the vision for the bilateral partnership, as outlined in the Joint
Statement of 13 February 2025.
(b) and (c) The Executive Orders signed by the U.S. President cover multiple areas
including U.S. foreign policy goals, U.S. participation in UN agencies, reciprocal
tariffs, illegal migration, national security etc. Some of the executive orders such as
those on immigration could have implications for Indian nationals in the U.S. The
concerned Ministries / Departments of Government of India are seized of the matter
and remain engaged with their U.S. counterparts to address these issues.
(d) and (e) As far India-U.S. trade is concerned, during the visit of Prime Minister
Modi to the United States on 12-13 February 2025, both India and the U.S. resolved
to deepen the bilateral trade relationship to promote growth that ensures fairness,
28.03.2025 169
national security and job creation. To this end, the two leaders set a new bilateral
trade target – "Mission 500” – aiming to more than double the total two-way goods
and services trade to $500 billion by 2030. Recognising that this level of ambition
would require new, fair-trade terms, both sides announced plans to negotiate the
first tranche of a mutually beneficial, multi-sector Bilateral Trade Agreement (BTA)
by the fall of 2025.
ONE NATION ONE PORT
4615. SHRI P P CHAUDHARY:
SHRI BHARTRUHARI MAHTAB:
SHRI BASAVARAJ BOMMAI:
SHRI YOGENDER CHANDOLIA:
SHRIMATI KAMALJEET SEHRAWAT:
DR. BHOLA SINGH:
SHRI PRAVEEN PATEL:
SHRI PRADEEP KUMAR SINGH:
SHRI DAMODAR AGRAWAL:
SHRI BIDYUT BARAN MAHATO:
SHRI DINESHBHAI MAKWANA:
SHRI ARUN GOVIL:
SHRI KOTA SRINIVASA POOJARY:
28.03.2025 170
DR. HEMANT VISHNU SAVARA:
SHRI LUMBA RAM CHAUDHARY:
SHRI DILIP SAIKIA:
SHRI JANARDAN MISHRA:
SUSHRI KANGNA RANAUT:
Will the Minister of PORTS, SHIPPING AND WATERWAYS be pleased to state:
(a) whether the Government has launched 'One Nation One Port' initiative;
(b) if so, the details thereof and the manner in which this new initiative aims to
eliminate inconsistencies in documentation, processes and streamlining
operations across India's major ports along with the timeline for its complete
integration;
(c) whether any assessment has been conducted regarding the projected cost
savings for stakeholders through this initiative, if so, the estimated reduction in
logistics costs and processing time thereof;
(d) whether any digitization and automation systems have been developed for
streamlining port documentation and processes, if so, the details thereof;
(e) whether any performance metrics have been established to evaluate the
effectiveness of this initiative, if so, the details thereof and improvements
observed so far; and
(f) the steps taken by the Government to enhance efficiency and global
competitiveness in the maritime sector in the country?
28.03.2025 171
THE MINISTER OF PORTS, SHIPPING AND WATERWAYS (SHRI
SARBANANDA SONOWAL):
(a) to (c) Yes.Ministry of Ports, Shipping and Waterways has launched ‘One Nation
One Port Process (ONOP)’ initiative which focuses on mapping and standardizing port
processes and documentation. This involves conducting a comprehensive review of
the existing procedures and documents exchanged within the port value chain across
all major ports which cover various cargo types, including containers, dry bulk, and
liquid bulk, as well as different movement categories such as export-import,
transshipment, and coastal operations. The implementation of ONOP initiative will
reduce the processing time of documentation which will lead to a reduction in logistics
cost.
(d) The Ministry of Ports, Shipping and Waterways has prioritized the digitization and
automation of port processes and documentation through the National Logistics Portal
– Marine (NLP-Marine) which also integrates the Enterprise Business System (EBS)
as the port operating system.
(e) A review has been conducted to assess the implementation of ONOP initiatives at
major ports which showed substantial improvement in port process documentation.
(f) Ministry of Ports, Shipping and Waterways has taken various steps to enhance
efficiency and competitiveness of ports in maritime sector which include development
of new ports, terminals and berths, modernization, mechanization, digitalization,
development of connectivity infrastructure for road and rail etc. Further
28.03.2025 172
“SagarAankalan” guidelines have been launched by the Ministry for enhancing
efficiency and competitiveness of Indian ports in the global maritime sector.
DEVELOPMENT OF INLAND WATERWAYS
4616. SHRI SELVARAJ V:
SHRI SUBBARAYAN K:
Will the Minister of PORTS, SHIPPING AND WATERWAYS be pleased to state:
(a) whether the Government has a plan to invest about Rs. 50,000 crore in the
development and improvement of Inland Waterways in the country in the coming
five years; and
(b) if so, the details of the areas where there is scope for development of waterways
and the steps that are being taken for the development and improvement of
inland waterways?
THE MINISTER OF PORTS, SHIPPING AND WATERWAYS (SHRI
SARBANANDA SONOWAL):
(a) and (b) Yes. Development works on National Waterways (NWs) are taken up by
Inland Waterways Authority of India (IWAI) with a view to develop NWs for cargo/river
cruise tourism wherever feasible. Main areas of development include fairway
development with navigable depth, construction of fixed and floating terminals/jetties
for berthing facilities and provision for suitable inland vessels.
28.03.2025 173

NEW NOTARY PORTAL
4618 SHRI TEJASVI SURYA:
SHRI SHRIRANG APPA CHANDU BARNE:
SHRI ANURAG SHARMA:
SHRIMATI BHARTI PARDHI:
SHRI BASAVARAJ BOMMAI:
SHRI MANOJ TIWARI:
SHRI DAMODAR AGRAWAL:
SHRIMATI MAHIMA KUMARI MEWAR:
SHRI KANWAR SINGH TANWAR:
SHRI TRIVENDRA SINGH RAWAT:
SHRI ARUN GOVIL:
SHRI SURESH KUMAR KASHYAP:
Will the Minister of LAW AND JUSTICE be please to state:
(a) whether the Government has launched a New Notary Portal for appointment
of Notaries and if so, the details along with the features thereof;
(b) the objective of creating the Notary Portal and the situation that existed before
its creation;
28.03.2025 175
(c) the role of the Notary Portal in promoting efficiency and transparency across
various States particularly in Amroha Parliamentary Constituency of Uttar
Pradesh;
(d) whether the Government proposes to implement additional features or
updates to further enhance the efficiency of the Notary Portal, if so, the details
thereof;
(e) whether any steps are being taken/proposed to be taken to create/develop a
digital storage facility for notary records; and
(f) if so, the details thereof along with the specific technology and infrastructure
being utilized to develop a secure digital storage facility for notary records and
the timeline for the complete digitization of notary records?
THE MINISTER OF STATE OF THE MINISTRY OF LAW AND JUSTICE; AND
MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF PARLIAMENTARY AFFAIRS (SHRI
ARJUN RAM MEGHWAL):
(a) to (d) The Government has launched the Notary Portal as a dedicated platform
for providing online services for the works related to the Notaries Act,1952 and the
Notaries Rules, 1956. It is intended to provide an online interface between the
Notaries and the Government for various services like submission of applications
for appointment as Notaries, verification of eligibility for appointment as Notaries,
issuance of digitally signed Certificate of Practice as a Notary, renewal of Certificate
of Practice, change of practice area, submission of annual return etc. The Notary
28.03.2025 176
Portal provides a faceless, paperless, transparent and efficient system. Presently,
only the module related to verification of documents and eligibility and issuance of
digitally signed Certificate of Practice to the newly appointed Notaries is live. Prior
to launch of the Notary Portal, Certificate of Practice used to be issued physically
to the Notaries. As on 25.3.2025, more than 26,600 digitally signed Certificates of
Practice have been issued to the newly appointed Notaries for various States and
Union Territories through the Notary Portal.
(e) No Sir.
(f) does not arise.
REJUVENATION OF INLAND WATERWAYS
4619. SHRI RAVINDRA DATTARAM WAIKAR:
DR. SHRIKANT EKNATH SHINDE:
SHRI RAJESH VERMA:
SHRIMATI SHAMBHAVI:
SHRI NARESH GANPAT MHASKE:
Will the Minister of PORTS, SHIPPING AND WATERWAYS be pleased to state:
(a) whether the Government has invested over Rs. 6,000 crores for the rejuvenation
of inland waterways since 2014 and if so, the details of the projects funded under
this initiative;
28.03.2025 177
(b) whether the number of National Waterways has increased from 5 to 111 under
the current administration and if so, the manner in which this expansion has
impacted cargo movement and passenger connectivity;
(c) whether the Government has set targets for cargo movement via inland
waterways, specifically aiming for 200 million MT by 2030 and 500 million MT
by 2047 and if so, the strategies that are being implemented to achieve these
goals;
(d) whether infrastructure projects in the country including Maharashtra are
expected to enhance trade and connectivity and if so, the details of the progress
made on these projects; and
(e) whether the revitalization of inland waterways is anticipated to contribute to eco-
tourism and sustainable transportation in India?
THE MINISTER OF PORTS, SHIPPING AND WATERWAYS (SHRI
SARBANANDA SONOWAL):
(a) Yes. An amount of Rs. 6434 cr. has been invested by the Government from 2014-
15 to 2023-24 for development of National Waterways (NWs) in the country. The
details of the projects funded are given at Statement-I.
(b) Yes. For development of Inland Water Transport (IWT) in the country, 111 National
Waterways were declared vide National Waterways Act, 2016. The cargo movement
on National Waterways has increased from 18 million ton per annum (MTPA) in 2013-
28.03.2025 178
14 to 133 MTPA in 2023-24 and passenger movement has reached to 1.61 Crores in
2023-24.
(c) Yes. The strategies to achieve the goals of 200 million MT by 2030 and 500 million
MT by 2047 in terms of Policy Measures are detailed at Statement-II.
(d) Yes. The inland water transport infrastructure development projects on National
Waterways across India including Maharashtra aims to enhance trade and
connectivity. The details of infrastructure facilities developed are at Statement-III.
(e) Yes. The inland water transport is economical, safe and environment friendly
mode of transportation. Revitalization of inland waterways is anticipated to contribute
to eco-tourism through River Cruises/House Boats and sustainable transportation.
