SHORTAGE OF TRAINED NURSING STAFF
4811.SHRI KHALILUR RAHAMAN:
Will the Minister of HEALTH AND FAMILY WELFARE be pleased to state:
(a) whether there is shortage of adequate trained nursing staff in the country;
(b) if so, the details thereof alongwith the estimated shortage; and
(c) the steps taken/proposed to be taken up by the Government to address the
shortage?
THE MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF HEALTH AND FAMILY
WELFARE; AND MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF CHEMICALS AND
FERTILIZERS (SHRIMATI ANUPRIYA PATEL):
(a) to (c) As per the Indian Nursing Council, there are around 38.49 lakh nursing
personnel registered in the country. The Nurse to Population ratio is approximately
2.20 nurses per 1000 population. There are around 5299 Nursing institutions
producing nearly 3.82 Lakh Nursing personnel annually in the country.
28.03.2025 1093
Some of the proactive measures taken to increase nursing personnel in the
country are as follows:-
(i) The Government has sanctioned the Centrally Sponsored Scheme
“Augmenting Nursing Education - Establishment of new Colleges of
Nursing in co-location with Medical Colleges” for establishment of 157
new nursing colleges in co-location with the existing medical colleges
established since 2014.
(ii) Under the Central Sector Scheme ‘Development of Nursing Services’
financial assistance is provided to States/UTs to upgrade Schools of
Nursing into Colleges of Nursing.
(iii) Distance between nursing educational institutions has been reduced
from 10 km to 5 km in Hilly areas.
(iv) Maximum distance between hospital and institution can be 50 km in Hilly
areas as against 30 km in plain areas.
(v) For Nursing Educational Programmes, student patient ratio has been
relaxed from 1:5 to 1:3.
(vi) The land requirement for Nursing Educational Institutes from 3 acres
has been relaxed to constructed building of 54,000 sq. ft. for
School/College of Nursing including Hostel.
(vii) To open GNM and B.Sc. (Nursing) programme, 100 bedded parent
hospital is essential since 2013-2014. However, this is relaxed for hilly
28.03.2025 1094
and tribal areas.
(viii) Norms for teaching faculty to start B.Sc. (N) Programme has been
relaxed as under:
 At least 2 M.Sc. (N) faculty to be available
 Qualification and Experience of the Nursing Teachers has been relaxed.
(ix) Super speciality Hospital can start M.Sc. (N) without having under
graduate programme. Further, student teacher ratio for M.Sc.(N)
programme has been relaxed from 1:5 to 1:10
(x) Age of superannuation for faculty in Nursing has been increased to 70
years.
(xi) INC has increased the maximum number of sanctioned seats for
Nursing programs to 300.
ENHANCEMENT OF REGIONAL STRATEGIC COOPERATION
4812 SHRI MUKESHKUMAR CHANDRAKAANT DALAL
Will the Minister of EXTERNAL AFFAIRS be pleased to state :-
(a) the initiatives undertaken by the Government to enhance regional strategic
cooperation particularly in the Gulf and Middle East and North Africa (MENA)
regions;
28.03.2025 1095
(b) the manner in which the India-Middle East-Europe Economic Corridor (IMEC)
is expected to improve connectivity and trade relations between India and
other countries;
(c) the measures being implemented to address security challenges in the region
and ensure maritime safety;
(d) the manner in which the Government plans to leverage its diplomatic efforts to
strengthen ties with countries in the Mediterranean and beyond; and
(e) the details of the role played by the Indian diaspora in fostering economic and
cultural connections between India and the regions discussed?
THE MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF EXTERNAL AFFAIRS; AND
MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF TEXTILES (SHRI PABITRA
MARGHERITA):
(a) and (d) With the objective to further strengthen ties with countries in Gulf,
MENA and Mediterranean region, India has leveraged its diplomatic efforts and
entered into multifaceted strategic partnerships with the several countries inter-alia
aimed at enhancing political ties, furthering diplomatic interests, strengthening
national security, foster greater economic and investments growth, securing energy
resources, deepening people-to-people ties, expanding cooperation in technology
and innovation, infrastructure development and increased global influence. The
details of India’s strategic partnerships with these countries is at Statement.
28.03.2025 1096
India is part of I2U2 (India, Israel, the UAE and the USA) initiative which is
aimed to encourage joint investments in six mutually identified areas such as water,
energy, transportation space, health and food security. It intends to mobilize private
sector capital and expertise to help modernize the infrastructure, decarbonise
industries, improve public health and promote the development of critical, emerging
and green technologies
(b) On the sidelines of the G20 Leaders’ Summit in New Delhi, the leaders of India,
European Union, France, Germany, Italy, Saudi Arabia, UAE and US announced
an MoU on 9 September 2023, committing to work together to develop a new India-
Middle East-Europe Economic Corridor (IMEEC). The IMEEC will comprise two
separate corridors, the east corridor connecting India to the Gulf and the northern
corridor connecting the Gulf to Europe. These corridors intend to enhance
connectivity, increase efficiency, reduce costs, secure regional supply chains,
increase trade accessibility, generate jobs and lower greenhouse gas emissions,
resulting in a transformative integration of Asia, Europe and Middle East.
An Intergovernmental Framework Agreement (IGFA) between India and the
United Arab Emirates (UAE) concerning cooperation for operation of the India-
Middle East-Europe Economic Corridor (IMEEC) was signed on 13 February 2024.
The main elements of the Framework include development and management of a
logistics platform, including a digital ecosystem, and provision of supply chain
services to handle all types of general cargo, bulk, containers and liquid bulk in
28.03.2025 1097
order to enable IMEEC. The implementation of the IMEEC involves multiple
stakeholders and is at an initial stage.
(c) The Government has a multi-pronged approach to address security challenges
in the region and ensure maritime safety. These, inter-alia, include Mutual and
Holistic Advancement for Security and Growth Across Regions (MAHASAGAR)
initiative, (an extension of SAGAR doctrine "Security and Growth for All in the
Region”), Indian Ocean Naval Symposium initiative, Indian Ocean Rim Association
(IORA), Colombo Security Conclave (CSC) and signing defence, security and
strategic agreements. India’s commitment to the well-being and progress of nations
of the Indian Ocean, including as first responder and a net security provider, is
based on the MAHASAGAR outlook, among others. India’s objective is to promote
greater prosperity in the region and of ensuring the Indian Ocean a free and open
space, respecting the UN Convention on the Law of the Seas (UNCLOS).
(e) Among the world’s largest overseas diaspora, we have brought about a
transformational change in its engagement with them. The 35.4 million strong
Overseas Indian community (15.9 million NRIs and 19.5 million PIOs) is an
invaluable partner in the progress of the nation. The Indian diaspora bolsters
economic and cultural connections, among others, by significantly contributing
through remittance, trade, investment, cultural exchanges, transfer of expertise and
knowledge thereby act as a bridge to further India’s national interest. A successful,
prosperous, and influential diaspora is an asset. India stands to gain a lot from
28.03.2025 1098
tapping its diaspora networks and productive utilization of the soft power that comes
from having such a flourishing diaspora. The Government’s efforts are also aimed
at harnessing the diaspora potential to its fullest.
28.03.2025 1099
STATEMENT-I
The details of countries with which India has leveraged its diplomatic efforts and entered into multifaceted
strategic partnerships
S. Name of India's strategic partnership with Objectives behind declaration of such strategic partnerships
No Country other countries since 2014
GULF
1 Kuwait Strategic Partnership During Prime Minister Narendra Modi’s visit to Kuwait in December 2024, India-Kuwait bilateral relationship
was elevated to Strategic Partnership.
To further strengthen bilateral partnership through comprehensive and structured cooperation in key areas,
including political, trade, investment, defence, security, energy, culture, education, technology and people-to-
people ties.
2 Qatar Strategic Partnership During the State Visit of visit of the Amir of Qatar Sheikh Tamim bin Hamad Al Thani to India, an Agreement on
the Establishment of Bilateral Strategic Partnership was signed on 18 February 2025,
in New Delhi.
The Agreement will deepen cooperation with Qatar in various fields including political, trade, energy,
investment, security, culture and people-to-people ties.
28.03.2025 1100
3 Saudi Arabia Strategic Partnership Council During Hon’ble Prime Minister’s visit to Riyadh, on 29 October 2019, strategic Partnership Council (SPC)
(SPC) Agreement Agreement was signed between India and Saudi Arabia, which established a high-level council to steer the
India-Saudi relationship.
The SPC Agreement aims to enhance the level of exchange of information between the two countries at the
strategic level and deepening of mutual political trust. It harnesses synergies between the development
strategies of the two countries and calls for enhancing cooperation between them in the political, economic,
commercial, security, military and cultural area.
4 UAE Comprehensive Strategic During the state visit of the then Crown Prince of Abu Dhabi, H.H. Sheikh Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan, to
India as Chief Guest at India’s 68th Republic Day Celebration, India and UAE signed the Comprehensive
Partnership - 2017
Strategic Partnership to further deepen, widen and strength the bilateral relationship and people – to – people
ties between two countires.
MENA Region
1 Egypt Strategic Partnership During the State Visit of President of the Arab Republic of Egypt, His Excellency Mr. Abdel Fattah El-Sisi to
India in January 2023, India and Egypt upgraded relations to Strategic Partnership.
It was to institutionalize a long-term commitment to the expanding, mutually beneficial and
multifaceted bilateral relations between India and Egypt including in political, development, economic,
trade, scientific, technological, defence, security & counter-terrorism.
2 Israel Strategic partnership During Prime Minister’s visit to Israel in July 2017, the two countries raised the bilateral relationship to
that of a strategic partnership. To build a broad based relationship to realise the full potential of
28.03.2025 1101
partnership in areas of development, technology, innovation, cyber security entrepreneurship, defence
and security.
MEDITERRANEAN
1 Greece Strategic Partnership India and Greece elevated the bilateral relations to the level of a Strategic Partnership in August 2023 during
the official visit of Hon’ble Prime Minister to Greece.
Building on the foundation of the long-standing warm and close relationship between their countries and
peoples, the Prime Ministers decided to upgrade India-Greece bilateral ties to the level of a "Strategic
Partnership” to strengthen bilateral cooperation across various areas, including political, security and economic
spheres.
2 Italy Strategic Partnership India and Italy relations were elevated to a strategic partnership in March 2023.
India considers Italy an important partner in Europe and the Mediterranean region. Italy is focusing on the Indo-
Pacific and considers India a key player in the region. The strategic partnership will infuse depth and momentum
across all domains of bilateral cooperation and strengthen our joint efforts to address global issues.
28.03.2025 1102

FIXED COMPONENT FOR DOMESTIC UREA PRODUCERS
4814 SHRI PARSHOTTAMBHAI RUPALA:
Will the Minister of CHEMICALS AND FERTILIZERS be pleased to state:
28.03.2025 1103
(a) the last time the Fixed Component for Domestic Urea producers was revised;
(b) whether the Government had set up a Committee to discuss the Fixed
Component issue for implementing the policy of Rs. 2300 per metric tonne,
as announced in 2014, for Rashtriya Chemicals & Fertilizers Ltd., National
Fertilizers Ltd., and KRIBHCO and cooperative urea producers a few years
ago;
(c) if so, the details of recommendations of the Committee and implementation
status thereof; and
(d) the details of correspondence with Department of Expenditure in this matter?
THE MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF HEALTH AND FAMILY
WELFARE; AND MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF CHEMICALS
AND FERTILIZERS (SHRIMATI ANUPRIYA PATEL):
(a) The Fixed Cost Component for Urea Units was last revised based on cost data
of 2002-03 under New Pricing Scheme-III notified on 08.03.2007. Thereafter, the
Department of Fertilizers issued Modified NPS-III on 2nd April 2014 for existing
Urea units, which includes payment of an Additional Fixed Cost of Rs.350/MT to all
the units; Special compensation of Rs.150/MT to Urea Plants that have completed
30 Years and Converted to Gas; and Minimum fixed cost of Rs. 2300/MT or actual
fixed cost prevailing during 2012-13, whichever is lower.
However, vide notification dated 30.03.2020, the provision of Minimum fixed
cost of Rs.2300/MT had been deleted from the Modified NPS-III while there was
no change in the rest of the provisions of Modified NPS-III.
28.03.2025 1104
(b) to (d) As per the observation of Expenditure Finance Committee (EFC) that the
issues related to the costing of Urea units may be referred to Chief Advisor (Cost)
in the Ministry of Finance for examination and recommendation, the Department of
Fertilizers requested Department of Expenditure that the Chief Advisor (Cost) may
examine and give recommendations on reinstating the provisions of the minimum
fixed cost of Rs. 2300/MT under the Modified NPS-III with retrospective effect i.e.
from 2nd April 2014. The matter is under examination.

ISSUE OF CRACKDOWN ON IMMIGRATION
4816. SHRI SACHITHANANTHAM R
Will the Minister of EXTERNAL AFFAIRS be pleased to state :-
(a) whether the Ministry has taken any ministerial level talks to address the issue
of immigration crackdowns and limiting H-1B visas; and
(b) if so, the details and the outcome thereof?
THE MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF ENVIRONMENT, FOREST
AND CLIMATE CHANGE; AND MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF
EXTERNAL AFFAIRS (SHRI KIRTI VARDHAN SINGH):
(a) and (b) Issues pertaining to mobility and migration are discussed from time to
time by Governments of India and the USA, including where necessary, at the
Ministerial level. Government also takes up concerns on streamlining legal mobility
of students and professionals, while facilitating short-term tourist and business
travel. Bilateral discussions also address illegal immigration and human trafficking
28.03.2025 1109
through strong action against bad actors, criminal facilitators, and illegal
immigration networks.
During the recent visit of Prime Minister Shri Narendra Modi to Washington
D.C., the two sides underlined the evolution of the world into a global workplace
and called for facilitating secure and mutually beneficial mobility frameworks
between the two countries.
STATUS OF AYUSH HOSPITALS AND DISPENSARIES
4817. DR. BYREDDY SHABARI:
Will the Minister of AYUSH be pleased to state:
(a) the total number of AYUSH hospitals and dispensaries currently operational in
the country, State/UT-wise;
(b) whether the Government has any plans to establish new AYUSH hospitals and
dispensaries particularly in underserved areas and if so, the details of approved
projects including timelines and fund allocations;
(c) the details of AYUSH hospitals and dispensaries that are currently being
expanded or upgraded including the scope of expansion, phase-wise progress and
expected timelines for its completion;
(d) the funds allocated, released and utilized for AYUSH hospitals and dispensaries
in the last five years, year and State-wise; and
(e) whether any challenges have been faced in the operation or expansion of
AYUSH healthcare facilities and if so, the steps taken to address them?
28.03.2025 1110
THE MINISTER OF STATE OF THE MINISTRY OF AYUSH; AND MINISTER OF
STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF HEALTH AND FAMILY WELFARE (SHRI
PRATAPRAO GANPATRAO JADHAV):
(a) The State/UT-wise list of total number of Ayush Hospitals and Dispensaries in
the country are enclosed as Statement-I.
(b) to (e) Public health being a State subject, establishment of new Ayush hospitals
and dispensaries particularly in underserved areas comes under the purview of
respective State Government. However, under Centrally Sponsored Scheme of
National Ayush Mission (NAM) there is a provision of financial assistance for
Setting up of 50/30/10 Bedded Integrated AYUSH Hospitals,construction of
building to establish new Ayush dispensary in the area where there are no Ayush
facilities available and for upgradation of Government/Panchayat/Government
aided Ayush Dispensaries. However, in this regard suitable proposal is required to
be submitted by the State/UT Governments through their State Annual Action Plan
(SAAP) as per the provisions of NAM guideline.
As per the proposals received from State/UT Governments,167 units of
Integrated Ayush Hospitals (IAHs) are approved in the country from 2014-15 to
2023-24. The State/UT wise status of approved IAHs is furnished as Statement-II.
Further State/UT wise status of new Ayush Dispensaries approved for
establishment is enclosed as Statement-III and State/UT-wise status of Ayush
hospitals and dispensaries supported for upgradation under NAM is enclosed as
Statement-IV. The State/UT-wise status of funds allocated, released and utilized
for upgradation of Ayush hospitals and dispensaries during last five years is
28.03.2025 1111
enclosed as Statement-V. Since, the implementation of Scheme comes under the
purview of respective State/UT Governments, timelines have not been fixed by the
Ministry.
Public Health being a State subject, the primary responsibility for operation
or expansion of Ayush healthcare facilities lies with the respective State/UT
Governments after resolving the challenges, if any.

PROGRESS OF SAGARMALA PROJECT IN ODISHA
4818. SHRI SUKANTA KUMAR PANIGRAHI:
Will the Minister of PORTS, SHIPPING AND WATERWAYS be pleased to state:
28.03.2025 1126
(a) the details of progress of the Sagarmala project in Odisha, including the
number of approved and completed projects;
(b) the amount of funds allocated, released and utilized for Sagarmala projects in
the said State along with the key benefits expected therefrom;
(c) the manner in which Odisha’s progress under the Sagarmala project is
compared to other States across the country in terms of implementation and
impact; and
(d) the measures taken/proposed to be taken by the Government to address legal,
land acquisition and forest clearance challenges to ensure smooth execution
of the project?
THE MINISTER OF PORTS, SHIPPING AND WATERWAYS (SHRI
SARBANANDA SONOWAL):
(a) and (b) Sagarmala is a flagship Central Sector Scheme of Ministry of Ports,
Shipping and Waterways (MoPSW)to promote port-led development in the country.
Under Sagarmala Scheme, MoPSWis partially funding 6 projects costing about
Rs.350Crore in the State of Odisha. The details of completed projects, funds
sanction and released of these projects are provided in Statement.
(c) Till date, the Ministry has undertaken 119 projectsof total cost ofRs. 9,406 Crore
across Coastal States / UTs. Out of these,6 projects costing aboutRs.350 Crore
are in the State of Odisha for partial funding. Sagarmala Scheme has embarked on
developing an ecosystem for promotion of Ro-Ro/ Ro-Pax Ferry urban waterway
transportation in the country including in the State of Odisha. These ferries reduce
logistics costs, improve trade efficiency, and provide a sustainable alternative to
28.03.2025 1127
road transport. They also boost coastal tourism by making remote destinations
more accessible, promoting eco-friendly travel options. The ease of access will
attract more domestic and religious tourists, benefiting local economies.
(d) In terms of Sagarmala Funding Guidelines, projects having all clearance in
place are considered for funding. And, to address the challenges faced by the
Implementing Agencies, Ministry periodically interacts with State Maritime Boards
(SMBs)/State Govts/UTs' Administration/ Central Line Ministries by organizing
project based and State level meetings to understand their concerns and aiding to
resolve their issues concerning Government of India in timely manner. Ministry from
time to time holds Maritime States Development Council (MSDC) meetings and
also encourage coastal States/UTs to hold State Sagarmala Committee meetings
for creating synergy among various stakeholders for expeditious implementation of
Sagarmala projects.

OPENING OF DEFENCE ROADS FOR PUBLIC ENTRANCE NEAR SAINIK
NAGAR
4819. SHRI JAGADISH SHETTAR:
Will the Minister of DEFENCE be pleased to state:
(a) the measures being initiated by the Government for opening of the Defence
Roads near Sainik Nagar, Chouglewadi-Papamala and Basav Colony
entrances which were utilized by the Public to reach Market/
Hospitals/Schools/Colleges since decades and are presently blocked for entry
to the public by the Defence authorities;
(b) whether these roads have been kept locked/blocked for public entry on the
grounds of security threat; and
28.03.2025 1129
(c) if so , the details thereof?
THE MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF DEFENCE (SHRI SANJAY
SETH):
(a) to (c) Following roads in Belgaum in the State of Karnataka have been closed
by Army Authorities considering Security threat to the defence establishments and
safety of civil population: -
(i) Sainik Nagar Road: The road traverses through the Military Area. An
alternate road exists for the residents of Sainik Nagar. The matter is
presently subjudice before Hon’ble High Court of Karnataka.
(ii) Chouglewadi-Papamala Road: The road is passing through the notified
Firing Range of Indian Army. Fencing of defence land has been undertaken
for security and safety of local population to avoid injuries during firing. An
alternate route is available for the residents.
(iii) Basav Colony Road: Army Authorities have reported that no such road
exists. An access through A-1 defence land was being used earlier by the
local public which was stopped due to security concerns. An alternate road
is already available for public use.
UPGRADATION OF GOVERNMENT HOSPITALS IN KERALA
4820. SHRI BENNY BEHANAN:
Will the Minister of HEALTH AND FAMILY WELFARE be pleased to state:
28.03.2025 1130
(a) the total amount of fund allocated by the Union Government for upgrading
Government hospitals and PHCs in Kerala during the last five years;
(b) whether there is any plan to establish new AIIMS like institutions in Kerala, if
so, the details thereof; and
(c) the initiatives being taken by the Government to improve healthcare access
in tribal and remote areas of Kerala?
THE MINISTER OF STATE OF THE MINISTRY OF AYUSH; AND MINISTER OF
STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF HEALTH AND FAMILY WELFARE (SHRI
PRATAPRAO GANPATRAO JADHAV):
(a) to (c) With the objective of attainment of universal access to equitable,
affordable and quality health care services, the Ministry of Health & Family Welfare
is implementing National Health Mission. Government provides technical and
financial support to the States/UTs including Kerala for improvement in health
infrastructure, availability of adequate human resources to health facilities, to
improve availability and accessibility to quality health care especially for the
underserved and marginalized groups.
The Ministry of Health and Family Welfare provides technical and financial
support to the States/UTs including Kerala to strengthen the public healthcare
system, based on the proposals received in the form of State Programme
Implementation Plans (SPIPs) under National Health Mission. Government of India
provides approval for the proposal in the form of Record of Proceedings (RoPs) as
per norms & available resources. The number of approvals granted to each State
28.03.2025 1131
including Kerala is referenced in the Record of Proceedings (RoPs) available at the
below mentioned link:
https://nhm.gov.in/index1.php?lang=1&level=1&sublinkid=1377&lid=744
The details of central release under the National Health Mission (NHM)
from the FY 2019-20 to FY 2024-25 to the State of Kerala is as follows:
(Rs. in Cr.)

28.03.2025 1132
Further, PM-Ayushman Bharat Health Infrastructure Mission (PM-
ABHIM) is one of the largest Pan India scheme with an outlay of Rs.64180/- Crores
for strengthening health care infrastructure to effectively manage and respond to
any future pandemics and outbreaks across the country. The scheme period is for
5 years i.e. FY 2021-22 to FY 2025-26.

Health Infrastructure Mission (PM-ABHIM) since inception to FY 2024-25 to the
State of Kerala is as follows:

The Ministry of Health & Family Welfare administers a Centrally Sponsored
Scheme (CSS) for ‘Strengthening and Upgradation of existing government medical
colleges for increasing PG seats and starting new PG disciplines’. Under phase-II
of this scheme, increase of 43 PG seats in Government Medical College Alappuza,
Government Medical College Ernakulam and Government Medical College
Kannur, have been approved on 08.08.2023 with an approved cost of Rs. 13.81 cr.
28.03.2025 1133
Central share amounting to Rs. 2.07 cr has been released to the state of Kerala till
date.
Under Pradhan Mantri Swasthya Suraksha Yojana (PMSSY), All India Institute
of Medical Sciences (AIIMS) are set up in different parts of the country in a phased
manner, keeping in view the gaps in availability of tertiary health care facilities. Till
date, 22 AIIMS have been sanctioned under the scheme. The proposal for
establishment of AIIMS in Kerala has not been approved in the current phase of
PMSSY.
Various initiatives carried out under NHM by the Government of India in various
States/UTs including Kerala in remote/tribal dominated areas of Kerela are
operationalisation of Ayushman Arogya Mandir, National Ambulance Services,
Mobile Medical Units, ASHAs, 24 x 7 Services and First Referral facilities, Prime
Minister's National Dialysis Programme, Free Diagnostics Service Initiative and
Free Drugs Service Initiative, various activities under Reproductive & Child Health,
Anemia Mukt Bharat (AMB) strategy, Pradhan Mantri TB Mukt Bharat Abhiyaan
(PMTBMBA) and Universal Immunization programme.
Under National Health Mission (NHM), tribal areas have following relaxed
norms addressing the need-based intervention in terms of healthcare infrastructure
and human resources:
i. The population norms for setting up health facilities in tribal areas have been
relaxed from 5,000, 30,000 and 1,20,000 to 3000, 20,000 and 80,000 for setting up
of Sub-Health Centre (SHC), Primary Health Centre (PHC) and Community Health
Centre (CHC) in tribal and hilly areas respectively;
28.03.2025 1134
ii. Against norm of one ASHA per 1000 population in normal areas, one ASHA
per habitation in Tribal/hilly and difficult areas; and
iii. Against the norm of 2 Mobile Medical Units (MMU) per district in plains, 4
MMUs per district in tribal/ hilly/ inaccessible/ remote and hard to reach areas.
Under the Pradhan Mantri Janjati Adivasi Nyaya Maha Abhiyan (PM-
JANMAN)launched on 15th November, 2023, further relaxation in NHM norms has
been provided to theParticularly Vulnerable Tribal Groups (PVTG) areas such as
10 MMU per district, additional ANM and Multi Purpose Centres (MPCs), and
beneficiary saturation in PVTG areas.
Dharti Aaba Janjatiya Gram Utkarsh Abhiyan (DA-JGUA)waslaunched
on 2nd October 2024 with an objective to improve socio-economic conditions and
provide basic facilities to tribal majority villages and villages with significant ST
population of Aspirational blocks.
HEALTHCARE FACILITIES IN FATEHGARH SAHIB
4821. DR. AMAR SINGH:
Will the Minister of HEALTH AND FAMILY WELFARE be pleased to state:
(a) whether the Government has any proposal to establish/upgrade healthcare
facilities, including hospitals and primary health centres in Fatehgarh Sahib of
Punjab, if so, the details thereof; and
(b) whether the Government has taken any measures to improve maternal and
child healthcare services in the Fatehgarh Sahib Parliamentary Constituency
of Punjab, if so, the details thereof?
28.03.2025 1135
THE MINISTER OF STATE OF THE MINISTRY OFAYUSH; ANDMINISTER OF
STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF HEALTH AND FAMILYWELFARE (SHRI
PRATAPRAO GANPATRAO JADHAV):
(a) Under the National Health Mission, the Ministry of Health and Family Welfare
provides technical and financial support to the States/UTs including State of Punjab
to strengthen the public healthcare system including establishment/upgradation of
healthcare facilities based on the proposals received in the form of Programme
Implementation Plans (PIPs) under National Health Mission. Government of India
provides approval for the proposal in the form of Record of Proceedings (RoPs) as
per norms & available resources. The details are available in public domain at:
https://nhm.gov.in/index4.php?lang=1&level=0&linkid=57&lid=70
The details of Construction/upgradation works approved under PM
Ayushman Bharat Health Infrastructure Mission (PM-ABHIM) and Fifteenth
Finance Commission (FC-XV) at the District Fatehgarh Sahib is enclosed as
Statement.
(b) Under National Health Mission (NHM), the Government of India has taken
various measures for improving maternal and child health services across all
States/UTs including district Fatehgarh Sahib district of Punjab as under:
 Janani Suraksha Yojana (JSY), a demand promotion and conditional cash
transfer scheme for promoting institutional delivery.
 Janani Shishu Suraksha Karyakram (JSSK) entitles all pregnant woman and
sick infants (up to one year of age) delivering in public health institutions to
have absolutely free and no expense delivery, including caesarean section.
28.03.2025 1136
The entitlements include free drugs, consumables, free diet during stay, free
diagnostics, free transportation and free blood transfusion, if required. Similar
entitlements are also in place for sick infants up to one year of age.
 Pradhan Mantri Surakshit Matritva Abhiyan (PMSMA) provides pregnant
women a fixed day, free of cost, assured and quality antenatal check up by a
Specialist/Medical Officer on the 9th day of every month.
 Extended PMSMA strategy ensures quality antenatal care (ANC) to pregnant
 women, especially to high-risk pregnant (HRP) women and individual HRP
tracking until a safe delivery is achieved by means of financial incentivization
for the identified high-risk pregnant women and accompanying Accredited
Social Health Activists (ASHAs) for extra 3 visits over and above the PMSMA
visit.
 LaQshya improves the quality of care in labour room and maternity operation
theatres to ensure that pregnant women receive respectful and quality care
during delivery and immediate post-partum.
 Surakshit Matritva Aashwasan (SUMAN) provides assured, dignified,
respectful and quality healthcare at no cost and zero tolerance for denial of
services for every pregnant woman and newborn visiting public health facilities
to end all preventable maternal and newborn deaths.
 Optimizing Postnatal Care aims to strengthen the quality of postnatal care by
laying emphasis on detection of danger signs in mothers and incentivization of
28.03.2025 1137
Accredited Social Health Activists (ASHAs) for prompt detection, referral &
treatment of such high-risk postpartum mothers.
 Village Health Sanitation and Nutrition Days (VHSNDs) are observed for
provision of maternal and child health services and creating awareness on
maternal and child-care including nutrition in convergence with Ministry of
Women and Child Development.
 Outreach camps are provisioned for improving the reach of health care
services especially in tribal and hard to reach areas. This platform is used to
increase the awareness for the Maternal and Child health services, community
mobilization as well as to track high-risk pregnancies.
 Facility Based New-born Care: Special New-born Care Units (SNCUs) are
established at District Hospital and Medical College level, New-born
Stabilization Units (NBSUs) are established at First Referral Units (FRUs)/
Community Health Centres (CHCs) for care of sick and small babies.
 Kangaroo Mother Care (KMC) is implemented at facility and community level
for low birth weight/ pre-term babies. It includes early and prolonged skin-to-
skin contact with the mother or family member and exclusive and frequent
breastfeeding.
 Community Based care of Newborn and Young Children: Under Home Based
New-born Care (HBNC) and Home-Based Care of Young Children (HBYC)
program, home visits are performed by ASHAs to improve child rearing
practices and to identify sick new-born and young children in the community.
28.03.2025 1138
 Social Awareness and Actions to Neutralize Pneumonia Successfully (SAANS)
initiative implemented since 2019 for reduction of childhood morbidity and
mortality due to Pneumonia.
 STOP Diarrhoea initiative is implemented for promoting use of ORS and Zinc
and for reducing morbidity and mortality due to childhood diarrhoea.
 Rashtriya Bal Swasthya Karyakram (RBSK): Children from 0 to 18 years of age
are screened for 32 health conditions (i.e. Diseases, Deficiencies, Defects and
Developmental delay) under Rashtriya Bal Swasthya Karyakram (RBSK) to
improve child survival. District Early Intervention Centres (DEICs) at district
health facility level are established for confirmation and management of
children screened under RBSK.
 Anemia Mukt Bharat (AMB) strategy is implemented to reduce anemia among
six beneficiaries age group - children (6-59 months), children (5-9 years),
adolescents (10-19 years), pregnant and lactating women and in women of
reproductive age group (15-49 years) in life cycle approach through
implementation of six interventions via robust institutional mechanism.
 Nutrition Rehabilitation Centers (NRCs) are set up at public health facilities to
provide in-patient medical and nutritional care to children under 5 years
suffering from Severe Acute Malnourishment (SAM) with medical complications
with special focus on improving the skills of mothers and caregivers on timely,
adequate and appropriate feeding for children.
28.03.2025 1139
 Mothers’ Absolute Affection (MAA) Programme is implemented to improve
breastfeeding coverage which includes early initiation of breastfeeding and
exclusive breastfeeding for first six months followed by counselling on age-
appropriate complementary feeding practices.
 Lactation Management Centres: Lactation Management Units (LMU) are
facilities established to ensure the availability of Mother’s Own Milk or Donor
Human Milk for feeding of sick, preterm and low birth weight babies admitted
in Intensive Care Units.
 Under National Deworming Day (NDD) albendazole tablets are administered
in a single fixed day approach via schools and Anganwadi centres in two
rounds (February and August) to reduce the soil transmitted helminth (STH)
infestation among all children and adolescents (1-19 years).
Further, as per information provided by the State of Punjab, Fatehgarh district has
a dedicated 50-bedded facility to provide comprehensive maternal and child health
services.
STATEMENT
Construction/upgradation works at District Fatehgarh Sahib
PM Ayushman Bharat Health Infrastructure Mission (PM-ABHIM):
 One 50 bedded Critical care block (CCB) has been approved for the District
Fatehgarh Sahib in FY 22-23 for an amount of Rs. 2375.00 Lakhs (as per PM
ABHIM RoP).
28.03.2025 1140
 One Integrated Public Health Lab (IPHL) has been approved for the District
Fatehgarh Sahib in FY 25-26 for an amount of Rs.125.00 Lakhs (as per NHM
PMS Portal).
Fifteenth Finance Commission (FC-XV)
 Construction of 2 Building-less Sub centres (Brongajer SC & Mahadian SC) for
an amount of Rs. 28.90 Lakhs each has been approved in FY 21-22 (as per
NHM PMS Portal).
 Construction of 1 Building-less Sub centres (Khanian SC) for an amount of Rs.
28.95 Lakhs has been approved in FY 22-23 (as per NHM PMS Portal).
 Construction of One Block Public Health Unit (BPHU) at Sanghol for an amount
of Rs. 59.96 Lakhs has been approved in FY 21-22 (as per NHM PMS Portal).
 Construction of 2 Block Public Health Unit (BPHUs) at Chanarthal Kalan &
Nandpur Kalour for an amount of Rs. 61.96 Lakhs each has been approved in
FY 22-23 (as per NHM PMS Portal).
STATUS OF PMMV YOJANA
4822. SHRI BIDYUT BARAN MAHATO:
SHRI ARUN GOVIL:
Will the Minister of WOMEN AND CHILD DEVELOPMENT be pleased to state:
(a) the current status of the Pradhan Mantri Matru Vandana Yojana (PMMVY),
including the number of beneficiaries covered so far, the fund disbursement
details along with its implementation across various States and rural areas;
28.03.2025 1141
(b) the details of the impact of PMMVY on maternal and child health outcomes
indicating the manner in which the scheme has contributed to financial support
for pregnant women and lactating mothers particularly in underserved and rural
areas in these States including Jharkhand and Uttar Pradesh; and
(c) whether any changes or updates are being considered to improve its
effectiveness including extending coverage/ revising eligibility criteria/
increasing the amount of cash transfer to ensure better support for
beneficiaries?
THE MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF WOMEN AND CHILD
DEVELOPMENT (SHRIMATI SAVITRI THAKUR):
(a) to (c) Since 01.01.2017, the Ministry of Women and Child Development is
implementing Pradhan Mantri Matru Vandana Yojana (PMMVY) across the country
(except Odisha and Telangana) through the officials of Women & Child
Development and Health & Family Welfare Departments of States and UTs. The
PMMVY is a Centrally Sponsored Maternity Benefit Scheme under which cash
incentives of ₹5,000/- is provided directly to the Bank/Post Office account of the
beneficiary in Direct Benefit Transfer (DBT) mode for first child. The eligible
beneficiaries receive the remaining cash incentive, as per approved norms towards
maternity benefit under Janani Suraksha Yojana after institutional delivery, so that
on an average a woman gets ₹6,000/-. Cash incentive of ₹6,000/- is also provided
under PMMVY to eligible beneficiaries for second child subject to the second child
being a girl.
28.03.2025 1142
The current status of PMMVY, including the number of beneficiaries covered
so far, and the fund disbursement details across various States/ UTs is placed at
Statement.
With the introduction of umbrella Mission Shakti scheme from 1st April, 2022,
the number of instalments under PMMVY for the first child have been reduced from
three (3) to two (2). Further, maternity benefit under PMMVY is also extended for
the second child subject to second child being a girl. As a result of revision of
scheme, a new online portal, Pradhan Mantri Matru Vandana Yojana Software
(PMMVYSoft) was developed and launched in March, 2023. Under PMMVYSoft,
Aadhaar authentication through UIDAI is done digitally and National Payment
Corporations of India (NPCI) verification is also ensured so that funds are directly
transferred only to the DBT-enabled Aadhaar-seeded bank or post office accounts
of beneficiaries.
PMMVY is implemented in 34 States and UTs of the country. The
Government of Odisha is in the process of onboarding PMMVY in their State and
the Government of Telangana is not implementing PMMVY in their State. In 29
States and UTs, the scheme is implemented through the Women & Child
Development Departments, where applications are registered at the level of
Anganwadi Worker, and subsequent processing is done at the level of Supervisor,
Child Development Project Officer (CDPO) and State Nodal Officer. In the 5 States
of Andhra Pradesh, Gujarat, Maharashtra, Tamil Nadu and West Bengal, the
scheme is implemented through the Health & Family Welfare Departments, where
applications are registered at the level of Accredited Social Health Activist (ASHA),
28.03.2025 1143
and subsequent processing is done at the level of Auxiliary Nurse Midwife (ANM),
Medical Officer and State Nodal Officer. The registration, processing and day-to-
day monitoring of PMMVY is done by the respective State and UT Governments.
Further, to facilitate the beneficiaries for tracking the status of their
applications and payment, a ‘Track and Search’ feature is available on the PMMVY
portal to show real time location and status of Application. Also, a Grievance
redressal module has been implemented to lodge and track any PMMVY related
grievances by any beneficiary.
The Ministry conducts various awareness programs including those on
Maternity Benefits through SANKALP-Hub for Empowerment of Women (HEW)
scheme for all States/UTs. It includes various Information, Education and
Communication (IEC) and Behavior Change Communication (BCC) activities such
as Prabhat Pheri, Nukkad Natak, Newspaper Advertisements, Airing Radio Jingles,
Selfie Campaign, Door to Door Campaign, Community Programmes which are
conducted at field functionary levels. Further, the Ministry is also running special
campaign for registration of eligible beneficiaries in all the States and UTs under
PMMVY on a periodic basis.
As per the Special Bulletin on Maternal Mortality Ratio (MMR) released by
the Registrar General of India (RGI), the current MMR of the country is 97 per lakh
live births. The Maternal Mortality Ratio (MMR), as per the Sample Registration
System (SRS) from 2016-18 to 2018-20 for India, and for the State of Jharkhand
and Uttar Pradesh is given in the table below:

योजना ह ै और रा(cid:186)य/संघ रा(cid:186)य (cid:177)े(cid:253) म(cid:164) इस योजना के काया(cid:170)(cid:198)वयन के पहल े िदन स े ही सभी पा(cid:253) लाभाथ(cid:234)

STRENGTHENING HEALTHCARE INFRASTRUCTURE IN ASSAM
4825. SHRI AMARSING TISSO:
(a) the details of healthcare infrastructure including the number of Primary Health
Centres (PHCs) and district Hospitals in Assam, particularly in Karbi Anglong
and Dima Hasao districts, district-wise;
(b) whether the Government has any plans to upgrade medical facilities and
deploy additional healthcare professionals in remote tribal areas and if so, the
details thereof;
(c) the status of maternal and child healthcare programmes in Assam and the
measures taken to reduce maternal and infant mortality rates;
(d) whether the Government has conducted any studies on the prevalence of
malnutrition and lifestyle diseases in Assam and if so, the findings thereof;
and
(e) the funds allocated and utilized for public health initiatives in Assam during
the last five years?
THE MINISTER OF STATE OF THE MINISTRY OF AYUSH; AND MINISTER OF
STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF HEALTH AND FAMILY WELFARE (SHRI
PRATAPRAO GANPATRAO JADHAV):
(a) and (b) Public Health and hospital being a state subject, the primary
responsibility for planning, implementing, maintaining and to set up adequate
number of healthcare facilities as per requirement is of that State Government.
Under the National Health Mission, financial and technical support is provided to
States/UTs to strengthen their healthcare system based on proposals received
28.03.2025 1154
from States/UTs in their Programme Implementation Plan within their resource
envelop. The upgradation of medical facilities and deployment of additional
healthcare professionals as per requirement may be proposed in PIP by the states
including remote tribal areas. The details of healthcare infrastructure are available
at:
https://mohfw.gov.in/sites/default/files/Health%20Dynamics%20of%20India%20%
28Infrastructure%20%26%20Human%20Resources%29%202022-
23_RE%20%281%29.pdf
(c) and (d) The Ministry of Health and Family Welfare (MoHFW) supports all
States/UTs in implementation of Reproductive, Maternal, Newborn, Child,
Adolescent Health and Nutrition (RMNCAH + N) strategy under National Health
Mission (NHM) based on the Annual Program Implementation Plan (APIP)
submitted by States / UTs. Under NHM, Government of India implements various
Maternal Health Care Programs such as Janani Suraksha Yojana (JSY), Janani
Shishu Suraksha Karyakram (JSSK), Surakshit Matritva Aashwasan (SUMAN),
Pradhan Mantri Surakshit Matritva Abhiyan (PMSMA), Mothers’ Absolute Affection
(MAA), Setting up of Maternal and Child Health (MCH) Wings, Birth Waiting Homes
(BWH), Anaemia Mukt Bharat (AMB), Facility-Based Newborn Care, Kangaroo
Mother Care (KMC), Community-based care of Newborn and Young Children,
STOP Diarrhoea initiative, Nutrition Rehabilitation Centres (NRCs), and Universal
Immunization Programme (UIP). As per the information provided the State
Government of Assam, to reduce the prevalence of malnutrition in the state, 29
Nutrition Rehabilitation Centres (NRCs) are functional. The malnourished children
28.03.2025 1155
with medical complication are referred in the NRCs for treatment and management
of Severe Acute Malnourishment (SAM).
For preventing lifestyle diseases healthy eating is promoted through “Eat
Right India” movement of Food Safety and Standards Authority of India. The
Government of India focuses on health promotion and awareness generation
through countrywide movements like “Fit India Movement” where people are
encouraged to remain healthy and fit.
(e) Central Release under National Health Mission (NHM) and Pradhan Mantri
Ayushman Bharat Health Infrastructure Mission (PM-ABHIM) and 15th Finance
Commission to the State of Assam from FY 2019-20 to FY 2023-24 is as follows:
(Rs. in Crore)
S. Financial NHM PM-ABHIM 15th Finance
No. Year Commission
1 2019-20 1749.24 NA NA
2 2020-21 1807.48 NA NA
3 2021-22 1955.93 57.90 272.25
4 2022-23 1981.83 2.26 -
5 2023-24 2257.06 91.45 -
ESTABLISHMENT OF ANIIMS IN A&N ISLANDS
4826. SHRI BISHNU PADA RAY:
Will the Minister of HEALTH AND FAMILY WELFARE be pleased to state:
28.03.2025 1156
(a) the timeline set for the full-fledged establishment of Andaman & Nicobar
Islands Institute of Medical Sciences (ANIIMS) in A & N Islands as per the
Government of India orders and instructions at the time of conveying sanction
for the establishment of the said institution;
(b) the current status of the said medical college in all respects, including the civil
work for building construction, labs and procurement of medical instruments;
(c) the total project cost for establishment of the said medical college along with
the amount sanctioned and released by the Government so far and the
detailed expenditure incurred till date; and
(d) the manner in which the cost escalation is likely to be managed and persons
to be held responsible for the cost escalation in case the work is not completed
within the set time-frame and within the allocated project amount?
THE MINISTER OF STATE OF THE MINISTRY OF AYUSH; AND MINISTER OF
STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF HEALTH AND FAMILY WELFARE (SHRI
PRATAPRAO GANPATRAO JADHAV):
(a) to (c) Andaman and Nicobar Islands Institute of Medical Sciences (ANIIMS) was
approved under Phase-I of the Centrally Sponsored Scheme (CSS) for
‘Establishment of New Medical Colleges attached with existing district/referral
hospitals’ Scheme of the Ministry of Health &Family Welfare, Government of India
in the year 2015 at a cost of Rs.189 crores for up-gradation of the existing GB Pant
Hospital-a district hospital at Sri Vijaya Puram in A&N Islands. The entire central
share of Rs.113.40 crore towards the project has been released to the UT
Administration. All labs (Pathology, Microbiology, Biochemistry) in the existing
28.03.2025 1157
hospital are fully functional in an adjacent building. All the required essential
equipments are available, and all basic and essential tests are being done
routinely. The construction of new building is on.
(d) Expenditure in excess of the approved cost of the Medical College will be
borne by the UT Administration. Establishment of ANIIMS is a project, requiring a
phased approach, due to the challenging terrain, geographical constraints,
logistical issues, and workforce availability etc. affecting the project’s progress.

मजबूत करना, है।
(ख) योजनाओ ंके काया(cid:170)(cid:198)वयन के िलए रा(cid:186)य(cid:332)/संघ रा(cid:186)य (cid:177)े(cid:253)(cid:332) को िनिध जारी क(cid:236) जाती है, संसदीय
िनवा(cid:170)चन (cid:177)े(cid:253) या िजले को सीधे िनिध जारी नह(cid:233) क(cid:236) जाती है। िपछले पांच वष(cid:334) और चालू वष(cid:170) के दौरान
महारा(cid:213)(cid:367) रा(cid:186)य म(cid:164) उपय(cid:170)(cid:265)ु योजनाओ ं के तहत जारी/उपयोग क(cid:236) गई िनिधय(cid:332) का योजनावार, वष(cid:170)वार

ESTABLISHMENT OF DRY PORT IN NASHIK
4828. SHRI RAJABHAU PARAG PRAKASH WAJE:
Will the Minister of PORTS, SHIPPING AND WATERWAYS be pleased to state:
(a) whether the Government is aware of the long-pending proposal for
establishing a Dry Port in Nashik to strengthen export infrastructure for local
industries and farmers and if so, the status thereof:
(b) whether land acquisition problems have delayed the project, if so, the steps
being taken in coordination with the Government of Maharashtra and local
authorities to resolve these issues;
(c) whether the Ministry has assessed the economic benefits of the Nashik Dry
Port in supporting agri-exports like grapes, onions, and pomegranates and
boosting MSMEs, if so, the details thereof;
(d) whether the Government is considering a detailed roadmap with clear
timelines, budget allocations and private sector partnerships to expedite the
project; and
(e) if so, the details and expected completion timeline?
28.03.2025 1163
THE MINISTER OF PORTS, SHIPPING AND WATERWAYS (SHRI
SARBANANDA SONOWAL):
(a) and (b) Yes. The Multi Modal Logistics Park (MMLP) proposal is with Ministry
of Road, Transport & Highways (MoRTH) for approval of project cost. The land for
the project is acquired by Jawaharlal Nehru Port Authority (JNPA) and currently is
in possession of JNPA.
(c) Yes. The detailed Feasibility Study Report has been prepared by Ministry of
Road Transport & Highways containing the detailed market demand analysis
including demand of agri produce, export and imports.
(d) and (e) MMLP Nashik is a project on Public Private Partnership (PPP) - Design
Build Finance Operate Transfer (DBFOT) basis. The project has a completion
timeline of 3 years after obtaining necessary approvals.
AYUSH CENTRES IN TAMIL NADU
4829. SHRI K GOPINATH:
Will the Minister of AYUSH be pleased to state:
(a) whether the Government proposes to implement any special scheme for
establishment of AYUSH Hospitals/Centres for Ayurvedic and Homoeopathic
treatments in the country, State/UT-wise, particularly in Tamil Nadu;
(b) if so, the details thereof;
(c) whether the Government has received any proposal for establishing AYUSH
Hospitals/Centres in Tamil Nadu;
28.03.2025 1164
(d) if so, the details thereof, district-wise, particularly in Krishnagiri district of Tamil
Nadu;
(e) the action taken/proposed to be taken by the Government and the progress
made so far in this regard; and
(f) the details of the procedure adopted for establishing AYUSH Hospitals/Centres
in the country including Tamil Nadu?
THE MINISTER OF STATE OF THE MINISTRY OF AYUSH; AND MINISTER OF
STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF HEALTH AND FAMILY WELFARE (SHRI
PRATAPRAO GANPATRAO JADHAV):
(a) to (f) Ministry of Ayush is implementing Centrally Sponsored Scheme of National
Ayush Mission (NAM) through State/UT Governments and providing financial
assistance for setting up of 50/30/10 bedded Integrated Ayush hospitals (IAH) for
providing Ayush treatment facilities in the country including Tamil Nadu which also
includes Ayurvedic and Homeopathic systems. Accordingly State/UT Governments
including Tamil Nadu may avail financial assistance by submitting suitable
proposals through State Annual Action Plans (SAAPs). As per the proposals
received through SAAPs from State/UT Governments, 167units of IAH in the
country including 05 in Tamil Nadu State have been approved from 2014-2015 to
2023-2024. The State/UT-wise and district-wise status of approved IAH along with
its progress in the country including Tamil Nadu is enclosed as Statement. As far
as Krishnagiri district of Tamil Nadu is concerned no such proposal have been
received from State Government through SAAP.
28.03.2025 1165
Further, with regard to establishment of 50/30/10 bedded Integrated Ayush
hospitals there is a dedicated guidelines under NAM and accordingly it is required
that State/UT Governments including Tamil Nadu has to submit suitable
proposals through SAAPs to Ministry for further consideration and approval.
STATEMENT
The State/UT-wise and district-wise status of approved IAH along with its
progress in the country including Tamil Nadu

12.05 hrs
ADDITION OF NEW DIRECTION 1A TO DIRECTIONS BY THE SPEAKER
UNDER THE RULES OF PROCEDURE AND CONDUCT OF BUSINESS IN
LOK SABHA
SECRETARY GENERAL: Sir, I wish to lay on the Table a copy of Direction 1A
(Hindi and English versions) issued by the Speaker under the Rules of Procedure
and Conduct of Business in Lok Sabha.
In Chapter 1 of the “Directions by the Speaker, Lok Sabha”, after Direction 1, the
28.03.2025 1198
following Heading and Direction thereto shall be inserted:-
DAILY ALLOWANCE
1A. A Member shall be entitled to receive daily allowance for attending the
Session of the House.
Provided that no Member shall be entitled to receive the aforesaid allowance
unless a Member signs the register, maintained for this purpose by the Secretariat
of the House of the People on all the days (except intervening holidays for which
no such signing is required) of the Session of the House.
Provided further that the signature shall include attendance marked through
any electronic or biometric means approved by Speaker and register shall include
physical register or e-register generated through Software maintained for this
purpose.


INDIAN PORTS BILL, 2025
THE MINISTER OF PORTS, SHIPPING AND WATERWAYS (SHRI
SARBANANDA SONOWAL): Respected Speaker Sir, I rise to move for leave to
introduce a Bill to consolidate the law relating to ports, promote integrated port
development, facilitate ease of doing business and ensure the optimum utilization
of India’s coastline; establish and empower State Maritime Boards for effective
management of ports other than major ports; establish the Maritime State
Development Council for fostering structured growth and development of the port
sector; provide for the management of pollution, disaster, emergencies, security,
safety, navigation, and data at ports; ensure compliance with India’s obligations
under international instruments to which it is a party; take measures for the
conservation of ports; provide for adjudicatory mechanisms for the redressal of
port-related disputes; and address matters connected therewith or incidental
thereto.

SHRI K. RADHAKRISHNAN (ALATHUR): Hon. Speaker, Sir, I oppose the
introduction of the Indian Ports Bill, 2025. … (Interruptions)
Sir, this Bill encroaches upon the powers of the State Governments and
undermines the federal structure of the Indian Constitution. The Bill seeks to
centralize control over ports, which interferes with the distribution of powers
between the Union and the State Governments, as defined in the Seventh
Schedule of the Constitution. The Bill violates the State List Entry 31, Article 246(3),
Article 162, and Article 368. … (Interruptions)
Therefore, I urge upon the Government to withdraw this Bill or replace it with
another Bill. … (Interruptions)

PROF. SOUGATA RAY (DUM DUM): Sir, under Rule 72(1) of the Rules of
Procedure and Conduct of Business in Lok Sabha, I oppose the introduction of the
Indian Ports Bill, 2025.
The Indian Ports Act was enacted in 1908. At that time, all the ports were
28.03.2025 1206
under the Centre and under the ownership of the Central Government. But in recent
times, many private ports have come up and parts of the Government ports have
been leased to the private companies. … (Interruptions)
The present legislation does not appear to be adequate to control the private
ports. It has been proposed in the new Bill that a new adjudicating mechanism will
be set up. So far as the wages of port workers are concerned, they are finalised
through a tripartite mechanism in which the labour has an equal role. The present
Bill may take away the rights of the workers.
Hence, I oppose the adjudication and I oppose the Bill. … (Interruptions)

DR. BACHHAV SHOBHA DINESH (DHULE): Hon. Chairman Sir, thank you very
much for giving me this opportunity to speak. I want to draw your attention towards
the consumers and medical service providers tortured by private health insurance
companies. According to IRDAI report, the health insurance claim settlement ratio
between these companies and medical service providers was only 79 per cent
during 2022-23 and it was less than 62 per cent in 2023-24.
It is pertinent to mention here that after hospitalization, these companies
issue Authorization Letter for treatment and surgery to patients. It is mandatory to
deposit medical bills within 40 days after discharge. These companies usually
refuse to deposit the money with the hospitals and keep on querying about the
treatment and procedures done by the hospital. I came to know about such an
incident in my constituency. A patient had to undergo a hip replacement surgery
and the total cost was around Rs. 2.4 lakh. But, only Rs. 1.7 lakh was paid by the
health insurance company and the patient had to bear the burden of Rs. 70,000 on
his own even after authorization. Therefore, I request you to kindly look into it and
initiate an enquiry against these irresponsible, corrupt and careless health
insurance companies. Thank you.
SUSHRI MAHUA MOITRA (KRISHNANAGAR): Thank you, Mr. Chairperson, Sir.
I would like to draw the attention of the House to a very serious issue regarding the
Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023.
 English translation of the speech originally delivered in Marathi.
28.03.2025 1211
Section 44(3) of the DPDP Act completely overrides Section 8(1) (j) of the
RTI Act. The RTI Act had a provision whereby data could only be denied if it
pertained to personal data or if it pertained to any information that could not be
given to Parliament or to a person.
Now, with the new DPDP Act, these exemptions in the RTI Act have been
completely done away with. Now, the Government can deny all personal data and
all data blanketly to anyone asking for data, which means you will not be able to
obtain data on wilful loan defaulters or on anything that the Government does not
want you to access. This completely undermines the RTI Act.
Furthermore, there is a Section in the DPDP Act that states any group,
including journalists, activists, and political parties, that possesses data can now
be classified as a data fiduciary, subjecting them to all the obligations of a data
fiduciary. The Data Protection Board, which is completely made up by the
Government, has the power to impose fines ranging from Rs.250 crore to Rs. 500
crore. This will have a chilling effect on anyone seeking data.
The Act has been gazetted, but rules have not yet been framed. So, we
would really request the Government to re-look at it, and take it back to repeal
Section 44(3) of the DPDP Act. Thank you.

CAPTAIN BRIJESH CHOWTA (DAKSHINA KANNADA): Sir, I would like to bring
to the attention of this House, and to the attention of the Civil Aviation Ministry the
urgent need for land acquisition for Mangalore Airport. I seek the intervention of the
Union Minister. Through his intervention, I would ask him to enable the UDAN
Scheme for our cities, which is the embodiment of the idea of ‘Vikas Bhi, Virasat
Bhi'.
The Mangalore International Airport, which is operated by a private operator,
has sought additional land from the district administration, which has asked the
State Government to provide safety area for the runway. The State Government
has written to the Airports Authority of India in this respect. The State Government
is willing to facilitate the land acquisition process, and I have personally met the
Civil Aviation Secretary who had also agreed to this. But, this is where it is stuck
now. It was in September this has happened. While all the agencies in-principle,
and in their own capacity and spaces, have said that they are willing to make this
happen, there has not been any action initiated to facilitate this.
28.03.2025 1213
Sir, the bottleneck is that there is no instruction from the Airports Authority of
India to the private operator or the State Government. The next course of action
has not been communicated to any of the agencies involved. That is the need of
the hour. The Central Aviation Ministry needs to intervene and facilitate a dialogue
among all the agencies, and at the earliest, make the land acquisition a reality.
While on paper there might be a bureaucratic delay for this, but the airport's
expansion and safety are seriously hampered because of this.
I would seek the intervention of the Union Civil Aviation Ministry. Thank you,
Sir.

वाटर (cid:255)ोज(cid:179)े ट ह ै । इस (cid:255)ोज(cid:179)े ट के फे ज-1 का 53 परस(cid:164)ट काम हो चुका ह ै । फे ज-2 का 81 परस(cid:164)ट काम
हो चुका ह ै । यह लगभग 140 िकलोमीटर लागँ पाइपलाइन ह,ै जहां स े लोग(cid:332) को इस प(cid:229)रयोजना के

ADV. DEAN KURIAKOSE (IDUKKI): Thank you, Sir, for giving me this opportunity
28.03.2025 1214
to raise an important matter pertaining to my State of Kerala. Very recently, the
Government of Kerala has tried to introduce a black law against the common
people. The attitude of the Government of Kerala nowadays shows that they want
to impose a jungle raj in the State. Due to the large-scale agitation from the
opposition parties, they were forced to withdraw that controversial law. That is a
bitter experience for the people of Kerala.
In this regard, we conducted a peaceful agitation in my constituency. Our
demand was to reopen the Aluva-Munnar Road, which is the shortest way from
Kothamangalam to Munnar. But that road has been completely blocked by the
Forest Department without any serious reason. The issue is about the 11.5
kilometres distance between Pooyamkutty and Perumbankuthu. But the Forest
Department did not allow any transportation on that road.
The Government is filing baseless cases against common individuals in this
incident. They have filed cases against me and other representatives of the people
in that area. The Bishop Emeritus, Mar George Punnakottil, an 89 year-old spiritual
leader, also participated in the agitation with about 3000 other people belonging to
various communities. But the Forest Department filed unnecessary and baseless
cases against us. I have no problem about filing any forest or police case against
me. But they have also filed cases against the spiritual leader and other common
people. We cannot tolerate this kind of an attitude of the Government.
I urge upon the Government to issue necessary directions to the
Government of Kerala against the jungle raj that they want to impose in Kerala.
They are trying to curtail our rights. ....(Interruptions)

and also against our hon. Member of Parliament.

Sir, train services on the Tatanagar-Kharagpur-Howrah section of the South
Eastern Railway are being delayed unexpectedly. Due to this, the daily commuters
are facing severe problems. Several other important passenger trains, like Tata-
Howrah Steel Express, Howrah Ispat Express, and Howrah-Ghatshila MEMU
Express, are being delayed unusually. The daily commuters are facing deep
suffering as local and express trains are not running on scheduled time on the
 English translation of the speech originally delivered in Bengali.
28.03.2025 1216
Tatanagar-Kharagpur-Howrah section due to freight transport. I request the railway
authorities to intervene in this matter immediately to ensure timely train services.
Sir, the construction work of the dazzling Amrit Bharat Station at Jhargram
is nearing completion, for which I thank the Honourable Railway Minister. Sir, my
sincere appeal to the Honourable Railway Minister, with the completion of the
construction work of Amrit Bharat Station in Jhargram, trains like Ranchi-Howrah

would request to kindly consider this project with utmost importance.

demand to immediately begin the work on the proposed Bhadutala-Jhargram rail
route via Lalgarh.

SHRI HIBI EDEN (ERNAKULAM): Hon. Chairperson, Sir, I would like to draw the
attention of the House to a very important matter not just related to my constituency
but to the entire country. The Indian Army is the pride of our nation. Army officers
and soldiers in spite of the extreme weather conditions, work tirelessly to protect
our country. Most of them invest their entire life savings into their dream homes.
The Army Welfare Housing Organization is one of the biggest scams our country
has ever seen.
I seek to bring the attention of the esteemed House to an ongoing crisis faced
by the residents of the AWHO Chander Kunj Army Towers in Vytilla, Kochi due to
faulty construction which has led to a decision of demolition and reconstruction. A
High Court-appointed district committee headed by the District Collector,
Ernakulum has confirmed that the Rs. 175 crore proposal by the AWHO only
accounts for the demolition and reconstruction of two towers. Without paying all the
expenses arising out of the irresponsible and corrupt activities which led to this
situation, the rent for the flat owners of Tower-IIB and C has not been included,
creating serious financial distress for them.
Additionally, the expenses for transportation form the existing flats as well as
compensation for the interior modifications made by the residents remain
unaccounted for.
My demand is this. I urge the Government of India to take proper action, that
is, a CBI inquiry into the scam. Whoever is part of the scam, which led to demolition
28.03.2025 1223
of these flats, should be taken into account. This is not just in my constituency, this
is also in Pune Jai Jawan Awas Yojana, in Chennai 852 flats whose ownership is
not transferred, in Coimbatore 484 flats, and in Yelahanka, Bengaluru 28 flats. A
CBI inquiry should happen. Additionally, the amount incurred as expenses should
be given. No extra charge should be incurred by these flat owners. There are 208
families whose life and livelihood is lost. They should be taken into account. Proper
compensation should be given and flats should be handed over.
Thank you.
ADV. ADOOR PRAKASH (ATTINGAL): Sir, I would like to raise an issue regarding
non-release of Samagra Shiksha Abhiyan (SSA) funds to the State of Kerala. The
Union Government is withholding SSA funds for not signing MOUs for the
implementation of PM SHRI Scheme in the State.
Samagra Shiksha Abhiyan is the key programme intended to achieve
universal elementary education which guarantees free and compulsory education
for children aged 6 to 14. The delay in the Central release of SSA funds has created
issues in school infrastructure improvements, teacher training, and student-support
activities. Opposition-ruled States like Tamil Nadu and West Bengal are also facing
the same issue. The Parliamentary Standing Committee on Education has
recommended the Union Government to resolve the issue and release the pending
funds to the States. The total pending funds amount to over Rs. 859 crore for
Kerala, Rs. 2,152 crore for Tamil Nadu and Rs. 1,000 crore for West Bengal. The
Union Government should ensure that none of the States are placed in a
disadvantageous position for not accepting the NEP 2020 or the PM SHRI Scheme.
28.03.2025 1224
I request the Government to consider this issue on priority and take
necessary action at the earliest

request the Government to implement the Shabari Rail project immediately as it is
the most convenient mode of transport to the Sabarimala Shrine.
Sabarimala is one of the largest pilgrimage centres in India, attracting
millions of devotees in a short period of time. Every year, around two to three crore
devotees visit Sabarimala shrine during the pilgrimage season, which lasts from

mid-November to mid-January. Over 60 per cent of devotees rely on the Railways
to reach the shrine, with a significant majority from neighbouring States such as
Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh, and Telangana travelling by train to
undertake their pilgrimage. Therefore, making this project a reality has been a long-

(xii) Need to establish a Human-Wildlife Conflict Mitigation and Resource
Utilization Framework
ADV. DEAN KURIAKOSE (IDUKKI): Human-wildlife conflict is a growing threat,
especially in Kerala, leading to human fatalities, crop destruction, and property
damage. Existing mitigation measures remain reactive and inadequate, while
compensation schemes fail to address the escalating crisis. The Government must
urgently adopt a proactive and transparent framework to mitigate human-wildlife
28.03.2025 1237
conflict. The creation of a Wildlife Conflict Mitigation Fund (WCMF) is urgently
required to finance preventive infrastructure, victim compensation, conservation
research, and conflict-reduction programs. Increased funding is necessary for
precautionary measures. Innovative approaches like auctioning the animal remains
from hunting should be thought of. Additionally, with no existing vermin list, species
like wild boar, which cause severe damage, should be culled, and proceeds from
their hunting be used to strengthen WCMF. Initiatives like auctioning the meat for
human consumption should also be considered. The proceeds of such auctions
should fund the Wildlife Conflict Mitigation Fund (WCMF) A balanced, evidence-
based approach is crucial to protect lives, livelihoods, and biodiversity. I urge the
Government to establish a Wildlife Conflict Mitigation and Resource Utilization
framework and introduce necessary legislative and administrative measures to
ensure long-term, sustainable solutions.
(xiii) Need to include the work pertaining to preservation and maintenance
of water resources under operational framework of MGNREGA
SHRI K. C. VENUGOPAL (ALAPPUZHA): The severe weather conditions and
heat waves in the state of Kerala have precipitated need for cleansing of water
bodies together with its conservation, including canals, rivers and water storage
facilities. In order to mitigate these challenges and ensure the sustainability of water
resources, it is imperative to undertake immediate remedial actions. These should
include removal of silt and debris from water bodies together with dredging and
restoration, to preserve critical water bodies. Considering the vital importance of
environmental protection, rural development, and the provision of safe water, I urge
28.03.2025 1238
that these measures be incorporated into the operational framework of the
Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Program (MGNREGA)
and prompt implementation be done to secure the water needs of our rural
communities and safeguard long-term sustainability of water resources.
Additionally, while the MGNREGA programme already is running on a deficit of
Rs.9,754 crore, the Government made no hike in the budgetary allocation in Budget
2025-26. While the Centre insists that the MGNREGA is a demand driven scheme
and additional funds are given when required, no upward revisions were made.
Therefore, I also urge the ministry to increase the budget allocation for this rural
flagship Programme.
(xiv) Need to expedite implementation of Sabarimala Greenfield Airport
Project in Kottayam district, Kerala
SHRI ANTO ANTONY (PATHANAMTHITTA): I would like to draw the attention of
the Government to the urgent need for fast-tracking the implementation of the
Sabarimala Greenfield Airport in Kottayam district, Kerala. This project near
Erumeli helps to enhance connectivity to Sabarimala, one of the largest pilgrimage
destinations in the country. The Ministry of Civil Aviation has granted "Site
Clearance" in April 2023, and the Ministry of Environment, Forest & Climate
Change provided the Terms of Reference (ToR) in July 2023. Once operational,
the airport will significantly boost tourism, facilitate seamless travel for millions of
Sabarimala pilgrims, and promote economic growth in Pathanamthitta, Kottayam,
Alappuzha, Kollam districts and neighbouring regions. Additionally, these districts
have a high number of NRIs, for whom improved air connectivity is a pressing need.
28.03.2025 1239
The airport will also benefit parts of Tamil Nadu, ensuring better regional integration
and development. I urge the concerned Ministries to expedite the process to ensure
swift execution of this critical infrastructure project.

(xvi) Regarding problems being faced by rice millers in Punjab due to delay
in Paddy procurement in the State
SHRI AMRINDER SINGH RAJA WARRING (LUDHIANA): I am giving this notice
on the concerns and plight of rice millers and farmers of Punjab and to demand
immediate arrangements of Specials to transport rice from Punjab. We are
approaching March end and rice mills are still waiting to deliver their rice due to the
28.03.2025 1240
insufficient availability of space. As per the established systems, Punjab is
supposed to complete rice procurement by 31 March each year. This year, as per
the available records, only about 35-37% of the rice could have been delivered till
date and approximately more than 60% of rice is yet to be delivered by the millers
of Punjab. I demand immediate provisions of Specials to transport rice from Punjab
so that enough space is made available to the rice millers to deliver the pending
rice of the ongoing season i.e. 2024-25. If Government of India does not listen to
this demand of Specials for Punjab, then rice millers will be forced to bear great
financial losses as the next paddy season is about to start while ongoing
procurement cycle is incomplete. This delay will force Punjab into a crisis in the
coming year 2025-26 if appropriate arrangements are not made.
(xvii) Need to ensure enhanced compensation for acquisition of land of
farmers in Punjab by National Highways Authority of India
DR. AMAR SINGH (FATEHGARH SAHIB): We have recently been witness to
scenes of confrontation between farmers and Government of Punjab regarding the
possession of land acquired for NHAI projects. While one appreciates the need for
projects of national importance, the plight of individual losing his sole source of
income must be kept in mind. The resistance is in part because we are taking
something of great value and under compensating the affected farmer. We have
seen in Punjab in the last few years that land prices especially for prime agricultural
land continue to be among the highest in the country. However, as land acquisition
happens in accordance with the Collector rates which are typically lower then
market rates. This result in lower price for acquired land than had the individual
28.03.2025 1241
sold the land in private transaction. At the same time there is major issue of land
records with lands being still in name of a common elder family member but
possession and income being taken by separated family members. Since farmers
give up their sole income generating asset in national interest, I request
compensation awarded must kept 4-5 times of the prevailing market rates to ensure
they get what’s rightfully due for giving fertile land.

(xix) Need to regulate and promote AYUSH as mainstream
healthcare system
SHRI KRISHNA PRASAD TENNETI (BAPATLA): I wish to highlight the need for
expanding the role of AYUSH in mainstream healthcare. With rising health and
lifestyle challenges affecting our nation, from mental health issues to chronic
conditions like diabetes and hypertension, the potential of AYUSH cannot be
28.03.2025 1242
ignored. The National Mental Health Survey of 2015-16 revealed that 10.6% of
adults suffer from mental health disorders, while the ICMR-INDA study of 2023
shows that over 10 crores of our population are affected by diabetes. Furthermore,
studies like those published in The Lancet Global Health indicate alarming levels
of physical inactivity. In this context, AYUSH holds a key position in addressing
these challenges through preventive healthcare. AYUSH, with its holistic approach,
offers diverse tools for prevention and well-being. Ayurveda, for instance, tailors
treatments based on individual conditions. Yoga and meditation improve physical
flexibility, cognitive health, and emotional resilience. Unani and Siddha therapies,
grounded in herbal treatments, and Naturopathy, focusing on lifestyle changes,
contribute significantly to internal healing. Thus, I urge the Government to intensify
efforts to research and regulate AYUSH systems, ensuring they are standardized
and integrated alongside allopathy. Mainstreaming AYUSH will not only strengthen
preventive care but also promote a balanced, holistic healthcare system for India.
(xx) Need to extend time-limit for procurement of Soyabean from farmers by
NAFED in Maharashtra
(cid:174)ी ओम(cid:255)काश भूपालिसहं उफ(cid:170) पवन राजेिनबं ालकर (उ(cid:214)मानाबाद) : क(cid:164) (cid:254) सरकार क(cid:236) योजना
NAFED अंतग(cid:170)त महारा(cid:213)(cid:367) के उ(cid:214)मानाबाद (धारािशव) िजल,े लातूर िजले के औसा और िनलंगा तालकु ा
तथा सोलापरु िजले के बाश(cid:234) तालुका के कुल 44013 िकसान(cid:332) ने पंजीकरण कराया था । इनम(cid:164) से
15,749 िकसान(cid:332) से सोयाबीन खरीदा जा चुका है, जबिक शेष 28,264 िकसान(cid:332) से सोयाबीन अभी

(xxi) Need to address the problems being faced by Anganwadi workers
in the country
SHRI N. K. PREMACHANDRAN (KOLLAM): Anganwadi workers are the frontline
workers under the ICDS scheme. They are responsible for providing
supplementary nutrition’s, pre - school education and health check –ups etc. The
responsibilities of the Anganwadi workers are high but their wages and welfare
measures are meagre. They are not able to meet the primary essential requirement
out of this. Anganwadi workers are in protest in Kerala. The demand is for
increasing their wages, implement ESI, pension and other welfare measures. The
duties and wages of the Anganwadi workers are not proportionate. It is pertinent to
note that there is gender discrimination. The equality envisaged in constitution is
not complied in the case of women workers. The meagre wages of Anganwadi
workers and Asha workers amounts to gender discrimination. Hence, I urge upon
the Government to increase the wages, implement other welfare measures
including pension to the Anganwadi workers.
